Mention at least three specialized ECG compression algorithms. **Model Solution (7 marks):** **ECG-Specific Challenges (3 marks):** 1. **Non-stationarity:** ECG is quasi-periodic with abrupt QRS complexes amidst low-amplitude P and T waves. The signal's statistical properties change drastically within a single heartbeat, complicating prediction-based methods. 2. **Diagnostic Criticality:** Specific features (QRS width, ST-segment elevation, R-peak amplitude) are **diagnostically load-bearing**. Even small distortions can alter automated diagnosis (e.g., MI detection). PRD must remain < 9%. 3. **Low Entropy but High Sample Correlation:** Successive ECG samples are highly correlated (correlation coefficient ≈ 0.95), but the *information* is concentrated in narrow QRS complexes (~100 ms out of ~1000 ms cycle). Compression must preserve these transients. **Why JPEG is Unsuitable (2 marks):** - JPEG uses **8×8 block-based DCT**, which introduces **blocking artifacts** at block boundaries. For ECG, these artifacts can mimic ST-segment changes → **false MI diagnosis**. - JPEG does not account for **inter-beat correlation**. ECG has redundant cycles; JPEG treats each block independently. - JPEG is optimized for natural images with smooth gradients. ECG has sharp QRS peaks, causing **Gibbs phenomenon (ringing artifacts)**. **Specialized ECG Algorithms (2 marks):** | Algorithm | Type | CR | Key Idea | |---|---|---|---| | **AZTEC** (Amplitude Zone Time Epoch Coding) | Lossy/Lossless hybrid | 3–10 | Extracts plateaus and slopes | | **TP (Turn Point) Algorithm** | Lossy | ≈ 2 | Reduces sampling to local extrema only | | **Fan Algorithm** | Lossy | ≈ 3 | Encodes signal as fans between min/max envelopes | | **DWT + SPIHT** | Lossy | 8–20 | Wavelet + Set Partitioning in Hierarchical Trees | | **LMS Adaptive Prediction** | Lossless | ≈ 2.5 | Linear predictor + entropy coding | --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams:** > > 1. **Forgetting the ×100% in PRD** — Many students compute the ratio but omit the percentage conversion. KTU examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this. > > 2. **Confusing CR with Compression Percentage** — CR = $B_{orig}/B_{comp}$ (a ratio), while CP = $(1 - B_{comp}/B_{orig}) \times 100\%$ (a percentage). These are NOT interchangeable. > > 3. **Mixing up PRD with PRDN** — PRD is sensitive to baseline offset; PRDN normalizes it. If a question says "ECG with baseline drift," use PRDN. > > 4. **State "clinically acceptable" with a number** — Vague answers like "low error" score zero. Always cite **PRD < 9%** explicitly. > > 5. **Huffman Code = Length must match the tree** — Re-derive the tree on paper before writing the table. A common error is giving C a length of 2 instead of 3. > > 6. **For SNR units** — SNR is in **dB** (decibels). Writing 16.99 without "dB" loses 0.5 marks. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:** - ✅ **CR** = $B_{orig}/B_{comp}$ → Dimensionless ratio, >1 = compression achieved. - ✅ **PRD** = $\sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (x[n]-\hat{x}[n])^2}{\sum x[n]^2}} \times 100\%$ → **<9%** is clinically acceptable. - ✅ **SNR** = $10\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\sum x[n]^2}{\sum (x[n]-\hat{x}[n])^2}\right)$ dB → **>30 dB** is high quality. - ✅ **MSE** = $\dfrac{1}{N}\sum (x[n] - \hat{x}[n])^2$ → Squared signal units. - ✅ **Lossless** = PRD = 0%, perfect reconstruction; uses Huffman, Arithmetic, RLE, LZW. - ✅ **Lossy** = PRD > 0%, bounded error; uses DCT, DWT + thresholding, Vector Quantization. - ✅ **DWT-based compression** is the *de facto* standard for modern ECG compression. - ✅ **JPEG is unsuitable for ECG** due to blocking artifacts that mimic ST changes. - ✅ **Huffman coding** produces prefix-free codes; $\bar{L} \geq H$ always. - ✅ **Shannon Entropy** $H = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ is the theoretical minimum bits/symbol. - ✅ **ECG-specific algorithms**: AZTEC, TP (Turn Point), Fan, SPIHT, LMS Prediction. - ✅ **Pipeline**: Pre-processing → Transform → Quantization (lossy only) → Entropy Coding. - ✅ **Trade-off curve**: As threshold $\tau$ increases, CR increases but PRD also increases — find the knee. - ✅ **MIT-BIH database** is the standard benchmark for ECG compression evaluation.

topics.title: Perform spectral modelling and analysis of PCG signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML507 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Perform spectral modelling and analysis of PCG signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a PCG Signal?

The **Phonocardiogram (PCG)** is a graphical recording of the acoustic vibrations produced by the heart during the cardiac cycle, captured using an electronic stethoscope, microphone, or phonocardiographic transducer placed on the chest wall. The PCG captures the audible heart sounds (**S1**, **S2**, and optional **S3**, **S4**) along with murmurs, clicks, and other pathological acoustic events.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A PCG signal is a non-stationary, quasi-periodic biomedical time-series signal whose time-frequency characteristics directly reflect the mechanical activity of the heart valves (mitral, tricuspid, aortic, pulmonary).

### 1.2 What is Spectral Modelling and Analysis?

**Spectral modelling** refers to the representation of a signal as the output of a parametric linear system driven by white noise or known excitations, characterized by a small set of parameters (poles, zeros, gains). **Spectral analysis** is the decomposition of the signal into its constituent frequency components to identify dominant frequencies, energy distribution, and resonant structures.

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 PCBML507 lab framework, Experiment 2 specifically trains students to extract a **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** representation of PCG signals and fit a parametric model (typically Auto-Regressive - AR) to characterize the frequency content of heart sounds.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Heart as a Musical Instrument"

Imagine a violin being played in a concert hall. The violin's body acts as a **resonant filter** that amplifies certain frequencies (its formants) based on its physical construction. If you record the violin's sound and perform spectral analysis, the resulting spectrum tells you about the violin's shape, wood density, and air cavity — its **physical identity**.

Similarly, the heart is like a biological instrument. The heart valves, chambers, blood flow, and surrounding tissues act as a **biological filter** that shapes the acoustic vibrations. When we perform spectral modelling on a PCG signal, we are essentially "listening" to the **resonant identity** of the heart:

- **S1 (Lub):** Caused by mitral and tricuspid valve closure — produces low-frequency energy around **20–150 Hz**.
- **S2 (Dub):** Caused by aortic and pulmonary valve closure — produces higher frequency energy around **50–200 Hz** with sharper transients.
- **Murmurs:** Pathological turbulent blood flow — produce sustained spectral content between **100–600 Hz**, often with harmonic structures.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Hook:** A normal heart sound is a clean chord; a pathological heart sound has "extra noise" extending into higher frequencies. Spectral analysis turns the doctor's ear into a quantifiable visual fingerprint.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics in PCG Spectral Analysis

- **Sampling Frequency (Fs):** Typically **44100 Hz**, **8000 Hz**, or **1000 Hz** depending on the application.
- **Frequency Band of Interest:** **20 Hz – 1000 Hz** (audible cardiac acoustics).
- **AR Model Order (p):** Typically **8–14** for PCG signals.
- **Normal Heart Rate:** **60–100 BPM** (1–1.67 Hz fundamental cardiac frequency).
- **S1 Duration:** **0.10 – 0.16 seconds**.
- **S2 Duration:** **0.08 – 0.14 seconds**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of PCG Spectral Analysis

The PCG signal $x[n]$ is a non-stationary signal whose statistical properties change between systole and diastole. Spectral analysis aims to extract the time-invariant frequency characteristics of each cardiac event by segmenting the signal into quasi-stationary windows.

#### Step 1: Pre-processing the PCG Signal
- **Bandpass Filtering:** Remove baseline wander (DC and very low frequencies < 20 Hz) and high-frequency noise (> 1000 Hz).
- **Normalization:** Scale the amplitude to unit variance or peak-to-peak unit range.
- **Segmentation:** Divide the signal into frames corresponding to S1, systole, S2, and diastole using the ECG reference or envelope-based segmentation.

#### Step 2: Non-Parametric Spectral Estimation (Welch's Method)
The **Welch method** estimates the Power Spectral Density (PSD) by averaging periodograms of overlapping windowed segments:

$$
\hat{P}_{xx}(f) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=0}^{M-1} \left| \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x_m[n] \, w[n] \, e^{-j2\pi f n} \right|^2
$$

Where $x_m[n]$ is the $m$-th segmented frame, $w[n]$ is the window function (Hamming/Hann), $L$ is the segment length, and $M$ is the number of segments.

#### Step 3: Parametric Spectral Modelling (AR Model)
The **Auto-Regressive (AR)** model assumes $x[n]$ is the output of an all-pole filter driven by white noise $e[n]$:

$$
x[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, x[n-k] + e[n]
$$

The AR PSD is then given by:

$$
P_{AR}(f) = \frac{\sigma_e^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi f k T} \right\vert^2}
$$

Where $\sigma_e^2$ is the variance of the driving white noise, $a_k$ are the AR coefficients, and $p$ is the model order.

#### Step 4: Model Order Selection
The **Akaike Information Criterion (AIC)** or **Minimum Description Length (MDL)** criteria are used:

$$
AIC(p) = N \ln(\sigma_e^2) + 2p
$$

$$
MDL(p) = N \ln(\sigma_e^2) + p \ln(N)
$$

The optimal $p$ minimizes the chosen criterion.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Master These for KTU 2024 Lab Viva and ESE**

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| AR Process | $x[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x[n-k] + e[n]$ | $e[n] \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_e^2)$ |
| AR PSD | $P_{AR}(f) = \sigma_e^2 / \vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi f k T}\vert^2$ | Watts / Hz |
| Yule-Walker Equations | $\mathbf{R} \mathbf{a} = \mathbf{r}$ | $\mathbf{R}$ is Toeplitz autocorrelation matrix |
| Levinson-Durbin Recursion | Computes $a_k$ efficiently | Complexity $O(p^2)$ |
| Welch PSD | $\hat{P}_{xx}(f) = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{m=0}^{M-1}\vert X_m(f)\vert^2$ | Variance $\propto 1/M$ |
| AIC Criterion | $AIC(p) = N \ln(\sigma_e^2) + 2p$ | Penalty per parameter = 2 |
| MDL Criterion | $MDL(p) = N \ln(\sigma_e^2) + p \ln(N)$ | Stronger penalty for large $N$ |
| Frequency Resolution | $\Delta f = F_s / L$ | Hz; $L$ = FFT length |
| Heart Rate from PCG | $HR = 60 / T_{RR}$ | BPM; $T_{RR}$ = cardiac period (sec) |
| S1 Frequency Range | **20 – 150 Hz** | Main mitral/tricuspid closure |
| S2 Frequency Range | **50 – 200 Hz** | Aortic/pulmonary closure |
| Murmur Frequency Range | **100 – 600 Hz** | Pathological |
| Normal Heart Rate | **60 – 100 BPM** | Adult resting |
| Sampling Theorem | $F_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist criterion |

### 2.3 Engineering Utility of PCG Spectral Analysis

- **Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD):** Spectral features feed classifiers (SVM, CNN) to detect valvular diseases such as aortic stenosis and mitral regurgitation.
- **Wearable Cardiac Monitoring:** Real-time spectral feature extraction on low-power DSP chips.
- **Telemedicine:** Spectral fingerprints transmitted as compact features for remote cardiac assessment.
- **Heart Sound Segmentation:** Spectral envelopes separate S1/S2 from systole/diastole.
- **Biometric Authentication:** PCG spectral signatures are unique per individual (cardiac biometrics).
- **Phonocardiography Research Foundation:** All modern heart-sound benchmarks (PhysioNet Cinc 2016, Pascal Challenge) use spectral features.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world deployment example:** The StethoMe AI stethoscope uses AR-spectral features combined with deep learning to detect pediatric pneumonia and asthma via PCG analysis at home.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: AR Model and Yule-Walker Equations

**Given:** The AR(p) model

$$
x[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, x[n-k] + e[n]
$$

with $e[n]$ being zero-mean white noise of variance $\sigma_e^2$.

**Goal:** Derive the Yule-Walker equations relating AR coefficients to the autocorrelation function $r_{xx}[\tau]$.

**Step 1: Multiply both sides by $x[n-\tau]$ and take expectation:**

$$
E\{x[n] \, x[n-\tau]\} = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, E\{x[n-k] \, x[n-\tau]\} + E\{e[n] \, x[n-\tau]\}
$$

**Step 2: Recognize the autocorrelation terms:**

By definition, $r_{xx}[\tau] = E\{x[n] \, x[n-\tau]\}$. Since $e[n]$ is white noise uncorrelated with past inputs $x[n-k]$ for $k \geq 1$, the last term is zero for $\tau \geq 1$, and equals $\sigma_e^2$ for $\tau = 0$.

**Step 3: Write the Yule-Walker equations for $\tau = 1, 2, \ldots, p$:**

$$
r_{xx}[\tau] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, r_{xx}[\tau - k]
$$

**Step 4: Express in matrix form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix}
r_{xx}[0] & r_{xx}[-1] & \cdots & r_{xx}[1-p] \\
r_{xx}[1] & r_{xx}[0] & \cdots & r_{xx}[2-p] \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
r_{xx}[p-1] & r_{xx}[p-2] & \cdots & r_{xx}[0]
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
a_1 \\ a_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_p
\end{bmatrix}
=
-
\begin{bmatrix}
r_{xx}[1] \\ r_{xx}[2] \\ \vdots \\ r_{xx}[p]
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Solve for AR coefficients:**

$$
\mathbf{a} = -\mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{r}
$$

**Step 6: The Levinson-Durbin recursion exploits the Toeplitz structure of $\mathbf{R}$ to solve in $O(p^2)$ time** rather than $O(p^3)$ for direct inversion.

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Welch's PSD Estimate

**Goal:** Show why averaging periodograms reduces variance.

**Step 1: Define the periodogram of a finite-length signal $x[n]$, $0 \leq n \leq L-1$:**

$$
\hat{P}_{per}(f) = \frac{1}{L} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x[n] \, e^{-j2\pi f n} \right\vert^2
$$

**Step 2: The variance of a single periodogram is asymptotically $\sigma^4$ (it does not decrease with $L$).** This is the fundamental bias-variance problem of non-parametric PSD estimation.

**Step 3: Welch's modification — segment the data into $M$ overlapping frames of length $L$, window each frame, compute the periodogram of each, then average:**

$$
\hat{P}_{Welch}(f) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=0}^{M-1} \hat{P}_m(f)
$$

**Step 4: Variance reduction by factor of $M$:**

$$
\text{Var}\{\hat{P}_{Welch}(f)\} \approx \frac{1}{M} \text{Var}\{\hat{P}_{per}(f)\}
$$

**Step 5: Trade-off: increasing $M$ (more segments) reduces variance but decreases frequency resolution $\Delta f = F_s / L$ because each segment becomes shorter.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Optimal segment length rule for PCG:** Choose $L$ such that $\Delta f \approx 5$ Hz for adequate frequency resolution of heart sound formants. For $F_s = 8000$ Hz, this gives $L = 1600$ samples.

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Model Order Selection via AIC

**Step 1: For an AR(p) model fitted to $N$ samples, the likelihood function is:**

$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \prod_{n=1}^{N} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_e^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{e[n]^2}{2\sigma_e^2}\right)
$$

**Step 2: The log-likelihood is:**

$$
\ln \mathcal{L} = -\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma_e^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma_e^2}\sum_{n=1}^{N} e[n]^2
$$

**Step 3: Akaike's information criterion balances fit (first term) against complexity (second term):**

$$
AIC(p) = -2 \ln \mathcal{L}_{max} + 2p
$$

**Step 4: Substituting the maximum-likelihood estimate of variance (the residual variance):**

$$
AIC(p) = N \ln(\sigma_e^2) + 2p + \text{constant}
$$

**Step 5: The optimal order $p^*$ is the value of $p$ that minimizes $AIC(p)$:**

$$
p^* = \arg\min_{p} \left[ N \ln(\sigma_e^2(p)) + 2p \right]
$$

**Step 6: For PCG signals, the empirical optimal $p$ lies in the range 8–14** (lower for normal sounds, higher for murmurs with more complex spectral structure).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection Guide

| Scenario | Best Method | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Quick PSD visualization of PCG | Welch Periodogram | Simple, robust, real-time capable |
| Detecting narrow spectral peaks (valve clicks) | AR model | Higher frequency resolution for short data |
| Modeling murmurs (broadband) | ARMA / AR | Captures both poles and zeros |
| Time-varying analysis (S1 → S2 → S1) | STFT / Spectrogram | Shows spectral evolution over time |
| High-resolution feature extraction | MUSIC / Eigenvector | Super-resolution for sinusoids |
| Real-time wearable implementation | LPC (Levinson-Durbin) | $O(p^2)$ complexity, low memory |
| Heart sound classification | Mel-spectrogram + CNN | Modern deep learning approach |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PCG Signal Acquisition and Spectral Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Heart Acoustic Source] --> B[Electronic Stethoscope / Microphone]
    B --> C[Analog Bandpass Filter 20 to 1000 Hz]
    C --> D[ADC Sampling Fs 8000 Hz]
    D --> E[Digital Preprocessing]
    E --> F1[Highpass Filter Remove DC]
    E --> F2[Lowpass Filter Anti aliasing]
    E --> F3[Normalization]
    F1 --> G[Signal Segmentation]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H1[Welch PSD Estimation]
    G --> H2[AR Model Fitting]
    G --> H3[STFT Spectrogram]
    H1 --> I[Spectral Feature Extraction]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Feature Vector]
    J --> K[Classification CAD]
    K --> L[Diagnosis Output]
```

### 4.2 AR Model Block Diagram — Linear Time-Invariant System View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WN[White Noise e with variance sigma squared] --> SUM[+]
    SUM --> H[All Pole Filter 1 over A z]
    H --> OUT[PCG Signal x n]
    OUT --> DELAY1[z to power minus 1]
    OUT --> DELAY2[z to power minus 2]
    OUT --> DELAYp[z to power minus p]
    DELAY1 --> GAIN1[minus a1]
    DELAY2 --> GAIN2[minus a2]
    DELAYp --> GAINp[minus ap]
    GAIN1 --> SUM
    GAIN2 --> SUM
    GAINp --> SUM
```

### 4.3 Frequency-Band Decomposition of PCG Events

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PCG[PCG Signal] --> BAND1[20 to 60 Hz Band]
    PCG --> BAND2[60 to 150 Hz Band]
    PCG --> BAND3[150 to 400 Hz Band]
    PCG --> BAND4[400 to 1000 Hz Band]
    BAND1 --> EV1[Low frequency rumble - ventricular gallop S3 S4]
    BAND2 --> EV2[Valve closure main energy S1 S2]
    BAND3 --> EV3[Systolic murmurs mid range]
    BAND4 --> EV4[High frequency murmurs - regurgitation clicks]
```

### 4.4 Welch Method Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[PCG Signal x n] --> SEG[Segment into M Overlapping Frames]
    SEG --> WIN[Apply Hamming Window to each frame]
    WIN --> FFT[Compute FFT of each frame]
    FFT --> MAG[Compute magnitude squared]
    MAG --> AVG[Average over M frames]
    AVG --> PSD[Welch PSD Estimate Pxx f]
```

### 4.5 PCG Cardiac Cycle Annotation Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CY[Cardiac Cycle] --> S1[S1 - Lub]
    CY --> SYSTOLE[Systole]
    CY --> S2[S2 - Dub]
    CY --> DIASTOLE[Diastole]
    S1 --> S1F[Freq 20 to 150 Hz - Duration 0.10 to 0.16 s]
    S2 --> S2F[Freq 50 to 200 Hz - Duration 0.08 to 0.14 s]
    SYSTOLE --> SYSTOLEF[Quiet region - murmurs may appear 100 to 600 Hz]
    DIASTOLE --> DIASTOLEF[Quiet region - S3 S4 may appear 20 to 70 Hz]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank 2024]**
*Define the Phonocardiogram (PCG) signal and list its four main frequency components along with their typical frequency ranges.*

**Model Answer:**
The **Phonocardiogram (PCG)** is a graphical or digital recording of the heart sounds produced during the cardiac cycle, captured using a phonocardiographic transducer or electronic stethoscope. The four main frequency components are:

1. **S1 (First Heart Sound):** 20–150 Hz, duration 0.10–0.16 s, caused by mitral and tricuspid valve closure.
2. **S2 (Second Heart Sound):** 50–200 Hz, duration 0.08–0.14 s, caused by aortic and pulmonary valve closure.
3. **S3 (Third Heart Sound):** 20–70 Hz, low-frequency ventricular gallop.
4. **S4 (Fourth Heart Sound):** 20–60 Hz, atrial gallop, associated with stiff ventricles.

(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for frequency ranges)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank 2024]**
*State the Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) for AR model order selection and explain its physical significance in PCG spectral modelling.*

**Model Answer:**
The **Akaike Information Criterion (AIC)** for AR model order selection is given by:

$$
AIC(p) = N \ln(\sigma_e^2) + 2p
$$

where $N$ is the number of samples, $\sigma_e^2$ is the residual white noise variance after fitting an AR(p) model, and $p$ is the model order. The optimal order $p^*$ minimizes the AIC.

**Physical significance in PCG modelling:** The first term measures the **goodness-of-fit** (how well the AR model explains the PCG spectrum), while the second term **penalizes model complexity**. For PCG signals, optimal $p$ lies between 8 and 14 — too few poles miss the resonant structure of valve closures, while too many overfit to noise and model the high-frequency murmur content incorrectly.

(3 marks: 1 mark for formula + 2 marks for significance)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE Lab Courses follow Module-Level Internal Choice. Two alternatives provided below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Style]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Yule-Walker equations** for an AR(p) process. Show how the **Levinson-Durbin recursion** solves them efficiently. Apply this to a 4th-order AR model of a normal S1 heart sound and identify the dominant pole locations.

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Starting from the AR(p) definition:
$$x[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, x[n-k] + e[n]$$

Multiply both sides by $x[n-\tau]$ and apply expectation $E\{\cdot\}$:

$$E\{x[n] x[n-\tau]\} = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k E\{x[n-k] x[n-\tau]\} + E\{e[n] x[n-\tau]\}$$

Since $e[n]$ is white noise, it is uncorrelated with past samples, so the last term is zero for $\tau \geq 1$. By autocorrelation symmetry, we obtain:

$$r_{xx}[\tau] + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k r_{xx}[\tau - k] = 0, \quad \tau = 1, 2, \ldots, p$$

For $\tau = 0$: $r_{xx}[0] + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k r_{xx}[-k] = \sigma_e^2$

In matrix form $\mathbf{R} \mathbf{a} = -\mathbf{r}$, solved as $\mathbf{a} = -\mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{r}$.

**Levinson-Durbin (1 mark):** Exploits the Toeplitz structure of $\mathbf{R}$ using the recursion:
$$k_m = -\frac{r_{xx}[m] + \sum_{i=1}^{m-1} a_{m-1,i} \, r_{xx}[m-i]}{\sigma_{m-1}^2}$$
$$a_{m,i} = a_{m-1,i} + k_m \, a_{m-1,m-i}, \quad i = 1, \ldots, m-1$$
$$\sigma_m^2 = \sigma_{m-1}^2 (1 - k_m^2)$$
This reduces complexity from $O(p^3)$ to $O(p^2)$.

**Application (1 mark):** For a normal S1, the AR(4) poles are typically located near the unit circle in the z-plane at frequencies around 30–80 Hz (the dominant low-frequency resonant content of mitral/tricuspid closure), with a pair of complex-conjugate poles representing the main S1 envelope.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A PCG signal is sampled at $F_s = 8000$ Hz. Compute the **Welch PSD estimate** using 50% overlapping Hamming-windowed segments of length $L = 1000$ samples. Discuss the frequency resolution, variance reduction, and the trade-off involved.

**Model Solution:**

**Frequency Resolution (2 marks):**
$$\Delta f = \frac{F_s}{L} = \frac{8000}{1000} = 8 \text{ Hz}$$

This resolution is sufficient to distinguish the S1 (20–150 Hz) and S2 (50–200 Hz) dominant frequencies, as well as to resolve murmur harmonics separated by 8 Hz or more.

**Number of Segments (2 marks):** If the total signal length is $N$ samples, with 50% overlap, the number of segments is:
$$M = \frac{N - L/2}{L/2} = \frac{2N}{L} - 1$$
For example, for $N = 10000$ samples, $M = 19$ segments.

**Variance Reduction (2 marks):**
$$\text{Var}\{\hat{P}_{Welch}\} \approx \frac{1}{M} \text{Var}\{\hat{P}_{per}\}$$
With $M = 19$, variance is reduced by a factor of 19 compared to a single periodogram of length 10000.

**Trade-off (1 mark):** Increasing $L$ improves frequency resolution ($\Delta f \propto 1/L$) but reduces $M$, increasing variance. Decreasing $L$ reduces variance but worsens resolution. The optimal balance for PCG is $L \approx 1000$–$2000$ samples at $F_s = 8000$ Hz.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Style]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **non-parametric (Welch)** and **parametric (AR)** spectral estimation methods for PCG signals. Use a labelled block diagram of both methods and discuss their advantages and limitations.

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Welch (Non-parametric) | AR (Parametric) |
|---|---|---|
| Model assumption | None (data-driven) | Linear all-pole system |
| Frequency resolution | Limited by $F_s / L$ | Theoretically infinite (limited by model order) |
| Variance | Reduced by averaging | Depends on coefficient estimation accuracy |
| Computational cost | $O(L \log L)$ via FFT | $O(p^2)$ via Levinson-Durbin |
| Suitable for | Long PCG recordings | Short, quasi-stationary segments (S1, S2) |
| Limitation | Spectral leakage | Model order selection required |
| Robustness to noise | High | Moderate (needs sufficient data) |

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**
- **Welch:** [PCG] → [Segment + Overlap] → [Hamming Window] → [FFT] → [Magnitude²] → [Average] → [PSD]
- **AR:** [PCG] → [Compute Autocorrelation r] → [Solve Yule-Walker via Levinson-Durbin] → [AR Coefficients aₖ] → [Evaluate $\sigma_e^2 / \vert 1 + \sum a_k z^{-k}\vert^2$] → [AR PSD]

**Discussion (2 marks):**
- **Welch** is preferred for **initial visualization** of PCG spectra and for **noisy real-world recordings** where model assumptions may be violated.
- **AR** is preferred for **short segments** like individual S1 or S2 sounds (typically 100–200 ms), where the Welch resolution would be too coarse. The AR model can resolve closely-spaced formants critical for distinguishing valve pathologies.
- **Hybrid approach:** Use Welch for overall spectral overview, then fit AR models to segmented heart sound events for fine-grained feature extraction.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the steps involved in **PCG signal pre-processing** before spectral analysis. Why is **bandpass filtering (20–1000 Hz)** essential? Discuss the role of **windowing** in the Welch method.

**Model Solution:**

**Pre-processing Steps (3 marks):**

1. **DC Removal (Highpass Filter ~0.5 Hz):** Removes the baseline offset introduced by sensor contact and respiration.
2. **Bandpass Filtering (20–1000 Hz):** Isolates the cardiac acoustic range; removes low-frequency muscle and respiratory noise, and high-frequency electronic noise.
3. **Normalization:** Divides by the signal peak (or by standard deviation) to bring amplitudes to a comparable range, enabling cross-subject comparison.
4. **Segmentation:** Using ECG R-peak reference or envelope-based detection (Shannon energy, Hilbert envelope), the PCG is segmented into S1, systole, S2, diastole.

**Why 20–1000 Hz Bandpass is Essential (2 marks):**
- **Below 20 Hz:** Dominated by respiration, motion artifacts, and baseline wander. Including this masks the low-frequency heart sound components.
- **Above 1000 Hz:** Primarily electronic noise, microphone self-noise, and ambient acoustic interference. Heart sounds above 600 Hz are rare and typically pathological only in specific high-frequency murmurs.
- The 20–1000 Hz range covers the **complete cardiac acoustic bandwidth** identified in clinical phonocardiography literature.

**Role of Windowing in Welch (2 marks):**
- Windowing (e.g., **Hamming**) tapers the segment edges to zero, reducing **spectral leakage** caused by the implicit rectangular window when computing the FFT.
- A Hamming window has a main lobe width of $\approx 4\Delta f$ and side-lobe levels of about $-43$ dB.
- Without windowing, sharp transients at segment boundaries (e.g., the onset of S1) would smear energy across the entire spectrum, distorting PSD estimates.
- **Trade-off:** Windowing reduces spectral leakage but widens the main lobe, slightly reducing frequency resolution. The 50% overlap between segments compensates for the information loss at segment boundaries.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Lab ESE:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $F_s$ factor in frequency calculations.** When computing $\Delta f = F_s / L$, students often write $1/L$ in Hz without including the sampling rate.
> 2. **Confusing Welch variance with bias.** Welch reduces variance but actually *increases* bias slightly due to windowing. Both must be acknowledged.
> 3. **Incorrect AR PSD formula.** Many students write $P_{AR}(f) = \sigma_e^2 \cdot \vert 1 + \sum a_k e^{-j2\pi f k}\vert^2$ (multiplication) instead of the correct **division**. This is the most common single error — worth 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring model stability.** AR coefficients must yield poles **inside the unit circle** for a stable model. Forgetting to verify this loses marks.
> 5. **Confusing AIC and MDL penalty terms.** AIC uses $2p$, MDL uses $p \ln(N)$ — mixing these shows lack of conceptual clarity.
> 6. **Not specifying window type and overlap percentage** in Welch method description — a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 7. **Using non-CGPA-relevant Python code** instead of pseudocode or block diagrams in theory answers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — PCG Spectral Modelling & Analysis**

- **PCG** = Phonocardiogram; recording of heart sounds; sampled typically at 8 kHz; bandwidth 20–1000 Hz.
- **S1:** 20–150 Hz (mitral/tricuspid closure); **S2:** 50–200 Hz (aortic/pulmonary closure); **Murmurs:** 100–600 Hz.
- **Spectral analysis** decomposes a signal into its frequency components; uses both non-parametric (Welch) and parametric (AR) methods.
- **Welch method** = average of periodograms of overlapping windowed segments; reduces variance by factor $M$; resolution = $F_s / L$.
- **AR model** = $x[n] = -\sum a_k x[n-k] + e[n]$; all-pole filter driven by white noise; estimated via Yule-Walker / Levinson-Durbin.
- **AR PSD:** $P_{AR}(f) = \sigma_e^2 / \vert 1 + \sum a_k e^{-j2\pi f k T}\vert^2$ — division, not multiplication.
- **Yule-Walker equations:** $\mathbf{R} \mathbf{a} = -\mathbf{r}$; solved in $O(p^3)$ directly or $O(p^2)$ via Levinson-Durbin.
- **AIC:** $AIC(p) = N \ln(\sigma_e^2) + 2p$; **MDL:** $MDL(p) = N \ln(\sigma_e^2) + p \ln(N)$.
- **Optimal AR order for PCG:** $p = 8$ to $14$.
- **Pre-processing pipeline:** DC removal → Bandpass 20–1000 Hz → Normalization → Segmentation (S1/S2/systole/diastole).
- **Welch window choice:** Hamming ($-43$ dB side-lobe); 50% overlap is standard.
- **Hamming window main lobe width:** $\approx 4 \Delta f$ — limits frequency resolution.
- **Trade-off in Welch:** Long $L$ → high resolution, high variance; Short $L$ → low resolution, low variance.
- **AR advantages for PCG:** Better resolution on short segments; smooth spectra; compact feature representation.
- **Welch advantages for PCG:** No model assumption; robust to noise; easy to implement via FFT.
- **Nyquist criterion:** $F_s \geq 2 f_{max}$; for PCG with $f_{max} = 1000$ Hz, $F_s \geq 2000$ Hz (typically 8000 Hz used).
- **Heart rate range:** 60–100 BPM normal; 1.67 Hz to 1.0 Hz fundamental cardiac frequency.
- **S1/S2 segmentation aids:** Shannon energy envelope, Hilbert envelope, ECG R-peak reference.
- **Clinical use:** Valvular disease detection (aortic stenosis, mitral regurgitation), CAD systems, wearable cardiac monitors.
- **KDE benchmark datasets:** PhysioNet 2016 Cinc Heart Sound Challenge, PASCAL Heart Sound Challenge.
- **Modern extensions:** Mel-spectrogram + CNN, wavelet scalogram, deep transfer learning — all build on the classical AR/Welch foundations.
- **Stability check:** All AR poles must lie **inside the unit circle** in the z-plane.
- **Levinson-Durbin outputs:** Reflection coefficients $k_m$ (PARCOR) — useful for speech/PCG coding applications.
- **Spectral leakage** is controlled by window choice; rectangular window has $-13$ dB side-lobes (worst), Blackman-Harris has $-92$ dB (best).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of 50 Hz notch filter for ECG signal and display PSD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBML507 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Design of 50 Hz notch filter for ECG signal and display PSD # Design of 50 Hz Notch Filter for ECG Signal and Display PSD ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Notch Filter** (also called a **Band-Stop Filter** or **Band-Reject Filter**) is a digital filter designed to attenuate a very narrow band of frequencies while passing all other frequencies with minimal loss. In the context of biomedical signal processing, a **50 Hz Notch Filter** is specifically engineered to suppress the **50 Hz power-line interference** that contaminates ECG recordings due to electromagnetic coupling from AC mains supply. The **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** represents the distribution of signal power across frequency components and is mathematically defined as the Fourier Transform of the autocorrelation function of the signal (Wiener-Khinchin theorem). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: In India, the AC mains supply operates at **f₀ = 50 Hz**. In the United States, it is **f₀ = 60 Hz**. The notch filter must be precisely tuned to the local supply frequency. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — The Audio Headphone Story**: Imagine you are listening to a soft musical piece on headphones, but a constant low hum (50 Hz electrical interference) is annoying you. A notch filter acts like a **highly selective volume slider for that single hum frequency only** — it turns down ONLY that exact pitch (50 Hz) while leaving the entire song untouched. Every other musical note (heart's P-QRS-T waves) plays at its original volume. The **PSD is like a graph showing how loud each musical note is** — when your notch filter works correctly, you can visually verify that the "50 Hz note" has dropped dramatically in loudness while all other notes remain the same. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Parameters Used in 50 Hz Notch Filter Design**: > - **Notch Frequency (f₀) = 50 Hz** (India's standard power-line frequency) > - **Sampling Frequency (fₛ) = 360 Hz or 500 Hz** (typical for ECG) > - **Normalized Digital Frequency (ω₀) = 2πf₀/fₛ radians/sample** > - **Quality Factor (Q) = f₀/BW** where BW is the -3 dB bandwidth of the notch > - **Pole Radius (r) = 0.9 to 0.99** (controls sharpness of notch) ### 1.4 The 50 Hz Interference Problem in ECG The ECG signal has very small amplitudes (≈ **1 mV**) and is highly susceptible to **50 Hz power-line interference** through: 1. **Capacitive coupling** between the patient and the power lines 2. **Electromagnetic induction** in the electrode cables 3. **Ground loops** in the recording system The 50 Hz interference often has amplitude comparable to or larger than the ECG itself, severely distorting the QRS complex and ST segment — making accurate clinical diagnosis impossible. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Types of Notch Filters There are two primary approaches to implement a 50 Hz notch filter: | Aspect | FIR Notch Filter | IIR Notch Filter | |--------|------------------|------------------| | **Structure** | Non-recursive | Recursive | | **Stability** | Always stable | Stable if poles inside unit circle | | **Order Required** | Very high (≥ 100) for sharp notch | Low order (2nd order sufficient) | | **Phase Response** | Linear phase | Non-linear phase | | **Computational Cost** | High | Low | | **Memory** | More | Less | | **Preferred in ECG** | Rarely | **Yes (commonly used)** | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Preference**: The IIR notch filter is **strongly preferred** in biomedical applications because it achieves a sharp, narrow notch using only a **2nd-order structure**, making it computationally efficient for real-time wearable ECG devices. ### 2.2 IIR Notch Filter Design — Pole-Zero Placement Method The IIR notch filter is constructed by placing **zeros ON the unit circle** at the notch frequency and **poles INSIDE the unit circle** close to the zeros. This creates a deep null at ω₀ while maintaining unity gain elsewhere. ### 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Logic **Step 1**: Identify the notch frequency in the digital domain. $$\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi f_0}{f_s}$$ **Step 2**: Place conjugate zeros on the unit circle at the notch frequency. $$z_{1,2} = e^{\pm j\omega_0}$$ **Step 3**: Place conjugate poles inside the unit circle, close to the zeros (controlled by r). $$p_{1,2} = r \cdot e^{\pm j\omega_0}$$ **Step 4**: Form the transfer function from the zero-pole configuration. $$H(z) = \frac{(1 - e^{j\omega_0}z^{-1})(1 - e^{-j\omega_0}z^{-1})}{(1 - r \cdot e^{j\omega_0}z^{-1})(1 - r \cdot e^{-j\omega_0}z^{-1})}$$ **Step 5**: Simplify using Euler's identity to obtain the real-coefficient form. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---------------------|-------------------------|---------------| | 1 | Digital notch frequency | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{2\pi f_0}{f_s}$ | radians/sample | | 2 | Notch filter transfer function | $H(z) = \dfrac{1 - 2\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}$ | r ∈ [0.9, 0.99] | | 3 | Bandwidth (3 dB) | $BW = 2(1-r)$ | normalized freq. | | 4 | Quality factor | $Q = \dfrac{\omega_0}{BW}$ | dimensionless | | 5 | Pole radius for desired Q | $r = 1 - \dfrac{\pi \cdot f_0}{Q \cdot f_s}$ | sharp notch if r → 1 | | 6 | Filter coefficient b₀ (gain) | $b_0 = \dfrac{1+r^2 - 2r\cos(\omega_0)}{2(1-\cos(\omega_0))}$ | normalization | | 7 | Analog prototype (Butterworth) | $H(s) = \dfrac{s^2 + \omega_0^2}{s^2 + Bs + \omega_0^2}$ | 2nd order | | 8 | Bilinear transform | $s = \dfrac{2}{T} \cdot \dfrac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$ | T = 1/fₛ | | 9 | Pre-warping formula | $\Omega = \dfrac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{2}\right)$ | analog frequency | | 10 | Periodogram PSD | $P_{xx}(f) = \dfrac{1}{N}\left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] e^{-j2\pi fn} \right\vert^2$ | power/Hz | | 11 | Welch's PSD (averaged) | $\hat{P}_{xx}(f) = \dfrac{1}{M}\sum_{k=1}^{M}\dfrac{1}{L U}\left\vert X_k(f) \right\vert^2$ | variance ↓ | | 12 | Window energy | $U = \dfrac{1}{L}\sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w^2[n]$ | for Welch's | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industrial Applications of 50 Hz Notch Filtering**: > - **Clinical ECG Machines**: Removing mains hum from patient recordings > - **Holter Monitors** (24-hour ambulatory ECG): Continuous cleaning of recorded data > - **Patient Monitoring Systems** in ICUs: Real-time display of clean waveforms > - **Defibrillator ECG Channels**: Critical for rhythm analysis during resuscitation > - **Brain-Computer Interfaces (EEG)**: 50 Hz interference removal from neural recordings > - **EMG/EOG Bioinstrumentation**: Suppression of supply-induced noise The **PSD display** is essential in clinical engineering because it provides visual confirmation that the **50 Hz component has been successfully attenuated**, while the **fundamental ECG frequency components (0.5–40 Hz)** remain intact. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Standard IIR Notch Filter Transfer Function **Starting Point — Zero Placement on Unit Circle:** We require two zeros exactly on the unit circle at angles ±ω₀ so that the filter magnitude response becomes zero at ω = ω₀. $$z_{1,2} = e^{\pm j\omega_0} = \cos(\omega_0) \pm j\sin(\omega_0)$$ **Numerator (Zeros Contribution):** The numerator polynomial is constructed as (z − z₁)(z − z₂): $$\text{Num}(z) = (z - e^{j\omega_0})(z - e^{-j\omega_0})$$ $$\text{Num}(z) = z^2 - (e^{j\omega_0} + e^{-j\omega_0})z + e^{j\omega_0} \cdot e^{-j\omega_0}$$ $$\text{Num}(z) = z^2 - 2\cos(\omega_0)z + 1$$ Dividing both sides by z² to express in terms of z⁻¹: $$\text{Num}(z^{-1}) = 1 - 2\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + z^{-2}$$ **Denominator (Poles Contribution):** The poles are placed at p₁,₂ = r·e^(±jω₀) where 0 < r < 1. $$\text{Den}(z) = (z - r e^{j\omega_0})(z - r e^{-j\omega_0})$$ $$\text{Den}(z) = z^2 - r(e^{j\omega_0} + e^{-j\omega_0})z + r^2 e^{j\omega_0} e^{-j\omega_0}$$ $$\text{Den}(z) = z^2 - 2r\cos(\omega_0)z + r^2$$ In terms of z⁻¹: $$\text{Den}(z^{-1}) = 1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}$$ **Final Standard Form of the Transfer Function:** $$\boxed{H(z) = \frac{1 - 2\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation of the Frequency Response Magnitude Evaluating H(z) on the unit circle z = e^(jω): $$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1 - 2\cos(\omega_0)e^{-j\omega} + e^{-j2\omega}}{1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0)e^{-j\omega} + r^2 e^{-j2\omega}}$$ The **magnitude response** is: $$\vert H(e^{j\omega})\vert = \frac{\vert 1 - 2\cos(\omega_0)e^{-j\omega} + e^{-j2\omega}\vert}{\vert 1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0)e^{-j\omega} + r^2 e^{-j2\omega}\vert}$$ After algebraic simplification using the identity: $$\vert 1 - 2\cos(\omega_0)e^{-j\omega} + e^{-j2\omega}\vert = 2\vert \sin\!\left(\frac{\omega - \omega_0}{2}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{\omega + \omega_0}{2}\right)\vert$$ ### 3.3 Bilinear Transform Derivation for Notch Filter **Step 1**: Define the analog prototype transfer function for a notch filter: $$H_a(s) = \frac{s^2 + \omega_0^2}{s^2 + Bs + \omega_0^2}$$ where B is the bandwidth parameter. **Step 2**: Apply frequency pre-warping to map the digital notch frequency to the analog domain: $$\Omega_0 = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega_0}{2}\right)$$ For an ECG sampled at fₛ = 500 Hz (T = 0.002 s) with f₀ = 50 Hz: $$\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi \cdot 50}{500} = \frac{\pi}{5} = 0.2\pi \text{ rad/sample}$$ $$\Omega_0 = \frac{2}{0.002}\tan\!\left(\frac{0.2\pi}{2}\right) = 1000\tan(0.1\pi)$$ $$\tan(0.1\pi) = \tan(18°) \approx 0.3249$$ $$\Omega_0 = 1000 \times 0.3249 \approx 324.9 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 3**: Apply the bilinear transformation: $$s = \frac{2}{T}\cdot\frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$$ Substituting into the analog prototype: $$H(z) = \frac{\left(\frac{2(1-z^{-1})}{T(1+z^{-1})}\right)^2 + \Omega_0^2}{\left(\frac{2(1-z^{-1})}{T(1+z^{-1})}\right)^2 + B\cdot\frac{2(1-z^{-1})}{T(1+z^{-1})} + \Omega_0^2}$$ ### 3.4 PSD Computation Derivation — Welch's Method **Step 1**: The autocorrelation of a discrete signal x[n] is: $$r_{xx}[m] = \lim_{N\to\infty}\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]x[n+m]$$ **Step 2**: Apply the Wiener-Khinchin theorem: $$P_{xx}(f) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} r_{xx}[m]e^{-j2\pi f m}$$ **Step 3**: In practice, the periodogram estimate uses the magnitude squared of the FFT: $$\hat{P}_{xx}(f) = \frac{1}{N}\left\vert X(f) \right\vert^2 = \frac{1}{N}\left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{N-1}x[n]e^{-j2\pi fn} \right\vert^2$$ **Step 4**: Welch's modification divides the signal into overlapping segments: $$\hat{P}_{xx}^{Welch}(f) = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^{M}\left(\frac{1}{L \cdot U}\right)\left\vert X_i(f)\right\vert^2$$ where: - M = total number of segments - L = segment length - U = window energy normalization factor - X_i(f) = FFT of i-th windowed segment ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Pole-Zero Configuration Trade-offs | Pole Radius (r) | Notch Sharpness | Bandwidth (BW) | Stability | Distortion | Typical Use | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---| | r = 0.90 | Wide | High (BW ≈ 0.20 ω₀) | Very stable | Minimal ringing | Casual filtering | | r = 0.95 | Moderate | Medium (BW ≈ 0.10 ω₀) | Stable | Low ringing | Standard ECG | | r = 0.98 | Sharp | Narrow (BW ≈ 0.04 ω₀) | Marginally stable | Some ringing | Research-grade | | r = 0.99 | Very sharp | Very narrow (BW ≈ 0.02 ω₀) | Near instability | Noticeable ringing | Selective filtering | | r → 1.00 | Ideal | BW → 0 | Unstable | Ringing artifacts | Not realizable | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip**: The choice of **r = 0.95** is generally considered optimal for ECG notch filtering. It provides a sufficiently narrow notch to eliminate 50 Hz hum while avoiding excessive transient ringing that would distort the QRS complex. ### 3.6 Numerical Worked Example **Problem**: Design a 50 Hz IIR notch filter for an ECG signal sampled at **fₛ = 360 Hz** with pole radius **r = 0.95**. **Solution**: Step 1 — Compute normalized digital frequency: $$\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi f_0}{f_s} = \frac{2\pi \times 50}{360} = \frac{\pi}{3.6} \approx 0.8727 \text{ rad}$$ Step 2 — Compute cosine of ω₀: $$\cos(\omega_0) = \cos(0.8727) \approx 0.6428$$ Step 3 — Form the transfer function: $$H(z) = \frac{1 - 2(0.6428)z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2(0.95)(0.6428)z^{-1} + (0.95)^2 z^{-2}}$$ $$H(z) = \frac{1 - 1.2856 z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 1.2213 z^{-1} + 0.9025 z^{-2}}$$ Step 4 — Verify the notch depth at ω = ω₀: $$\vert H(e^{j\omega_0})\vert = \frac{\vert 1 - 2(0.6428)e^{-j\omega_0} + e^{-j2\omega_0}\vert}{\vert 1 - 2(0.95)(0.6428)e^{-j\omega_0} + (0.9025)e^{-j2\omega_0}\vert}$$ Since the numerator vanishes exactly at ω = ω₀ (zeros on unit circle), the magnitude at the notch is theoretically zero. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Overall Notch Filtering System for ECG ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["ECG SIGNAL
x(t) at fs Hz"] --> nodeB["ANTI-ALIASING
LOWPASS FILTER"] nodeB --> nodeC["ADC
SAMPLING"] nodeC --> nodeD["DIGITAL 50 HZ
NOTCH FILTER
H(z)"] nodeD --> nodeE["CLEANED ECG
SIGNAL y(n)"] nodeD --> nodeF["PSD ESTIMATOR
WELCH METHOD"] nodeF --> nodeG["POWER SPECTRAL
DENSITY PLOT"] nodeE --> nodeH["DISPLAY
AND ANALYSIS"] nodeG --> nodeH ``` ### 4.2 Direct Form II Implementation Structure ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeX["Input x[n]"] --> nodeA1["Sum Node (+b0)"] nodeA1 --> nodeZ1["Delay z-1"] nodeZ1 --> nodeZ2["Delay z-1"] nodeZ2 --> nodeA2["Output y[n]"] nodeA1 --> nodeM1["Multiply by -1.2856"] nodeM1 --> nodeA2 nodeZ1 --> nodeM2["Multiply by 1.2213"] nodeM2 --> nodeA1 nodeZ2 --> nodeM3["Multiply by -0.9025"] nodeM3 --> nodeA1 nodeA2 --> nodeZ1 ``` ### 4.3 Pole-Zero Plot on the Unit Circle (Z-Plane) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ZPLANE["Z-PLANE POLE-ZERO PLOT"] nodeC["ORIGIN
(0,0)"] --> nodeUC["UNIT CIRCLE
radius = 1"] nodeZ1["ZERO Z1
e to j omega_0
on unit circle"] nodeZ2["ZERO Z2
e to -j omega_0
on unit circle"] nodeP1["POLE P1
r e to j omega_0
inside circle"] nodeP2["POLE P2
r e to -j omega_0
inside circle"] nodeANNOT["Conjugate pairs symmetric
about real axis"] end ``` ### 4.4 Flow Chart — Experimental Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeS1["START
Load ECG Signal"] --> nodeS2["Add 50 Hz
Synthetic Noise"] nodeS2 --> nodeS3["Compute Parameters
omega_0 and r"] nodeS3 --> nodeS4["Design Notch Filter
H(z) coefficients"] nodeS4 --> nodeS5["Apply Filter to
Contaminated ECG"] nodeS5 --> nodeS6["Compute PSD via
Welch Method"] nodeS6 --> nodeS7["Compare PSD Before
and After Filtering"] nodeS7 --> nodeS8["Verify Notch Depth
at 50 Hz"] nodeS8 --> nodeS9["END - Validate
Clinical Quality"] ``` ### 4.5 Magnitude Response — Expected Shape ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph MAGRESP["EXPECTED MAGNITUDE RESPONSE of NOTCH FILTER"] nodeLF["LOW FREQUENCIES
Gain = 1 (0 dB)"] nodeM1["0.5 to 40 Hz
ECG PASSBAND"] nodeN["DEEP NOTCH at 50 Hz
Gain approaches 0"] nodeM2["60 Hz onwards
Gain returns to 1"] nodeHF["HIGH FREQUENCIES
Gain = 1 (0 dB)"] end nodeLF --> nodeM1 nodeM1 --> nodeN nodeN --> nodeM2 nodeM2 --> nodeHF ``` ### 4.6 PSD Computation Flow (Welch's Algorithm) ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeP1["Input Signal
x of length N"] --> nodeP2["Segment into K
overlapping frames"] nodeP2 --> nodeP3["Apply Window
Hamming or Hanning"] nodeP3 --> nodeP4["Compute FFT of
each windowed frame"] nodeP4 --> nodeP5["Compute Magnitude
Squared X_k of f"] nodeP5 --> nodeP6["Average across
all frames"] nodeP6 --> nodeP7["Normalize by
window energy U"] nodeP7 --> nodeP8["Final PSD Estimate
Pxx of f"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **What is a notch filter? Why is a 50 Hz notch filter specifically required in ECG signal processing?** **Model Answer (3 marks)**: A notch filter is a digital filter that attenuates a very narrow band of frequencies while passing all other frequencies with minimal attenuation. In ECG signal processing, a 50 Hz notch filter is required because the AC power-line supply in India operates at **50 Hz**, which gets inductively and capacitively coupled into the ECG recording electrodes and cables. This **50 Hz interference** has amplitude often comparable to the ECG signal itself (≈ 1 mV) and severely distorts the QRS complex and ST segment. The notch filter provides a sharp, narrow null precisely at **f₀ = 50 Hz**, effectively removing the power-line hum while preserving the diagnostic ECG frequency content (0.5–40 Hz). --- #### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Define Power Spectral Density (PSD). Mention the formula for the periodogram method of PSD estimation.** **Model Answer (3 marks)**: The **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** of a discrete-time signal x[n] is defined as the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) of its autocorrelation function rₓₓ[m], per the Wiener-Khinchin theorem. It describes how the signal power is distributed across frequency components, with units of **power per Hz**. The **periodogram method** computes PSD as: $$\hat{P}_{xx}(f) = \frac{1}{N}\left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{N-1}x[n]e^{-j2\pi fn} \right\vert^2 = \frac{1}{N}\vert X(f)\vert^2$$ where N is the signal length and X(f) is the DTFT (computed in practice via the FFT algorithm). --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice #### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a)** With the necessary equations, explain the pole-zero placement method for designing a 2nd-order IIR notch filter to eliminate 50 Hz interference from an ECG signal sampled at fₛ = 500 Hz. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss Welch's method for PSD estimation. Explain how PSD can be used to verify the performance of the designed 50 Hz notch filter on the ECG signal. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution**: **(a) Pole-Zero Placement Method (7 marks)**: The IIR notch filter is constructed by placing zeros ON the unit circle and poles INSIDE the unit circle at the notch frequency. **Step 1**: Compute the normalized digital notch frequency: $$\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi f_0}{f_s} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 50}{500} = 0.2\pi \text{ rad/sample}$$ **Step 2**: Place conjugate zeros on the unit circle: $$z_{1,2} = e^{\pm j\omega_0} = e^{\pm j0.2\pi}$$ **Step 3**: Place conjugate poles close to the zeros (using pole radius r): $$p_{1,2} = r\cdot e^{\pm j\omega_0} \quad \text{with } r = 0.95$$ **Step 4**: Form the transfer function: $$H(z) = \frac{1 - 2\cos(0.2\pi)z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2(0.95)\cos(0.2\pi)z^{-1} + (0.95)^2 z^{-2}}$$ Since cos(0.2π) ≈ 0.8090: $$H(z) = \frac{1 - 1.6180 z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 1.5371 z^{-1} + 0.9025 z^{-2}}$$ **Design Logic**: Zeros on the unit circle guarantee infinite attenuation at ω₀. Poles placed close to zeros (but inside) maintain a flat unity gain at all other frequencies. The radius r controls the notch sharpness — higher r gives a narrower notch but causes more transient ringing. **(b) Welch's Method for PSD (7 marks)**: Welch's method is an improved PSD estimation technique that reduces the variance of the standard periodogram through averaging. The procedure involves: **Step 1**: Divide the signal x[n] of length N into K overlapping segments of length L. **Step 2**: Apply a window function w[n] (typically Hamming or Hanning) to each segment: $$x_k^{(w)}[n] = x_k[n]\cdot w[n]$$ **Step 3**: Compute the FFT of each windowed segment: $$X_k(f) = \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x_k^{(w)}[n]e^{-j2\pi fn}$$ **Step 4**: Compute the periodogram of each segment and average: $$\hat{P}_{xx}^{Welch}(f) = \frac{1}{K\cdot L\cdot U}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\vert X_k(f)\vert^2$$ where U is the window energy normalization. **Verification of Notch Filter Performance**: By comparing PSD plots BEFORE and AFTER applying the notch filter: - **Before filtering**: A sharp peak appears at f = 50 Hz in the PSD plot - **After filtering**: The 50 Hz peak is suppressed by **30–40 dB** while the surrounding ECG frequency content (0.5–40 Hz) remains intact The depth of the PSD notch at 50 Hz is a direct quantitative measure of filter performance. A well-designed notch filter should provide ≥ 30 dB attenuation at f₀. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(a)** Derive the transfer function of a 2nd-order IIR notch filter using the pole-zero placement technique. Show that the filter provides zero magnitude at the notch frequency ω₀. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the concept of the Power Spectral Density (PSD). With suitable diagrams, describe the periodogram and Welch methods of PSD estimation, highlighting the variance reduction achieved by Welch's method. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution**: **(a) Derivation of IIR Notch Filter Transfer Function (7 marks)**: The notch filter design begins by specifying the notch frequency ω₀. To create a frequency null at ω₀, we place two complex conjugate zeros on the unit circle at z = e^(±jω₀). **Zeros on unit circle**: $$z_{1,2} = e^{\pm j\omega_0}$$ **Numerator construction**: $$N(z) = (z - e^{j\omega_0})(z - e^{-j\omega_0}) = z^2 - 2\cos(\omega_0)z + 1$$ To obtain unity gain at DC and Nyquist, we place conjugate poles at: $$p_{1,2} = r\cdot e^{\pm j\omega_0} \quad (0 < r < 1)$$ **Denominator construction**: $$D(z) = (z - re^{j\omega_0})(z - re^{-j\omega_0}) = z^2 - 2r\cos(\omega_0)z + r^2$$ **Transfer Function in z⁻¹ form**: $$\boxed{H(z) = \frac{1 - 2\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}}$$ **Proof of Zero Magnitude at ω = ω₀**: Substituting z = e^(jω₀) into H(z): $$H(e^{j\omega_0}) = \frac{1 - 2\cos(\omega_0)e^{-j\omega_0} + e^{-j2\omega_0}}{1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0)e^{-j\omega_0} + r^2 e^{-j2\omega_0}}$$ The numerator can be rewritten as: $$1 - 2\cos(\omega_0)e^{-j\omega_0} + e^{-j2\omega_0} = (1 - e^{-j\omega_0})(1 - e^{-j\omega_0}) = (1 - e^{-j\omega_0})^2$$ Since e^(-jω₀) ≠ 1 (because ω₀ ≠ 0), the numerator becomes zero: $$\vert H(e^{j\omega_0})\vert = 0 \text{ at } \omega = \omega_0$$ This proves the filter has infinite attenuation (zero magnitude) at the notch frequency. **(b) PSD Concepts and Methods (7 marks)**: **Concept of PSD**: The Power Spectral Density Pₓₓ(f) describes the average power distribution of a signal as a function of frequency. Per the Wiener-Khinchin theorem: $$P_{xx}(f) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} r_{xx}[m]e^{-j2\pi fm}$$ **Periodogram Method**: - Direct computation: take FFT of signal and compute magnitude squared - $\hat{P}_{xx}(f) = \frac{1}{N}\vert X(f)\vert^2$ - **Disadvantage**: High variance — does not improve with increasing N - Result is a noisy, fluctuating spectral estimate **Welch's Method**: - Divides signal into K overlapping segments (typically 50% overlap) - Applies window to each segment (Hamming/Hanning) - Computes FFT of each windowed segment - Averages the periodograms of all segments **Variance Comparison**: The variance of the standard periodogram is σ² ≈ Pₓₓ²(f), independent of N. For Welch's method with K non-overlapping segments, the variance reduces to: $$\text{Var}[\hat{P}_{xx}^{Welch}] \approx \frac{1}{K}\cdot P_{xx}^2(f)$$ This **1/K reduction** in variance is the key advantage of Welch's method, providing smoother and more reliable PSD estimates. The trade-off is reduced frequency resolution (segment length L < N). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**: > 1. **Common Mistake 1 (2 marks lost)**: Writing ω₀ = 50 Hz directly. The correct formulation is **ω₀ = 2πf₀/fₛ** in radians/sample. The 50 Hz is the analog frequency, while ω₀ is the normalized digital frequency. > 2. **Common Mistake 2 (2 marks lost)**: Placing poles ON the unit circle (r = 1). This makes the filter **unstable** and useless. Poles must always be strictly INSIDE the unit circle (r < 1). > 3. **Common Mistake 3 (2 marks lost)**: Confusing periodogram with Welch's method. The periodogram uses the entire signal at once; Welch's method **averages periodograms of multiple windowed segments**. > 4. **Common Mistake 4 (1 mark lost)**: Forgetting to mention the **normalization factor U** (window energy) in Welch's PSD formula. This is essential for unbiased PSD estimation. > 5. **Common Mistake 5 (1 mark lost)**: Not verifying the stability of the designed filter. Always state that poles must lie inside the unit circle for BIBO stability. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2 Experiment** - ⭐ **Notch Filter**: A band-stop filter with very narrow rejection band, used to remove a single specific interfering frequency. - ⭐ **50 Hz Interference**: Caused by capacitive/inductive coupling from AC mains supply; amplitude often comparable to ECG signal. - ⭐ **Standard Notch Frequency Formula**: $\omega_0 = \dfrac{2\pi f_0}{f_s}$ — Always convert analog frequency to digital normalized frequency first. - ⭐ **Pole-Zero Configuration**: Zeros ON unit circle (r = 1) at ω₀; poles INSIDE unit circle (r = 0.9–0.99) close to zeros. - ⭐ **Standard IIR Notch Transfer Function**: $H(z) = \dfrac{1 - 2\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}$ - ⭐ **Pole Radius r**: Controls notch sharpness. Higher r → narrower notch but more transient ringing. **r = 0.95** is optimal for ECG. - ⭐ **Filter Stability**: Requires all poles strictly inside the unit circle, i.e., r < 1. - ⭐ **Bilinear Transform**: $s = \dfrac{2}{T}\cdot\dfrac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$ — used to convert analog prototype to digital filter. - ⭐ **Pre-warping**: Required before bilinear transform to ensure correct frequency mapping. - ⭐ **PSD Definition**: DTFT of autocorrelation function; describes power distribution vs frequency. - ⭐ **Periodogram**: $\hat{P}_{xx}(f) = \dfrac{1}{N}\vert X(f)\vert^2$ — simple but high variance. - ⭐ **Welch's Method**: Segment signal → window each segment → FFT → average periodograms. Reduces variance by factor of K. - ⭐ **Window Energy Normalization**: Divide by U = (1/L)Σw²[n] for unbiased PSD estimate. - ⭐ **Verification**: Compare PSD plots before and after filtering; expect ≥ 30 dB attenuation at f₀ = 50 Hz. - ⭐ **Common Sampling Frequencies for ECG**: 360 Hz (standard MIT-BIH database), 500 Hz, 1000 Hz (high-resolution). - ⭐ **ECG Diagnostic Band**: 0.5 Hz to 40 Hz — must be preserved by the notch filter. - ⭐ **IIR is preferred over FIR for notch filters** because of computational efficiency and lower order requirements.

topics.title: Detection of events and waves of EEG signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBML507 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Detection of events and waves of EEG signals # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Electroencephalogram (EEG)** is a non-invasive electrophysiological recording technique that captures the summed post-synaptic electrical activity of cortical pyramidal neurons via scalp electrodes placed according to the international **10–20 system**. The resulting time-series voltage signal contains distinct **rhythmic oscillations** (termed *waves*) and transient **morphological events** (termed *events* or *graphoelements*). In the context of **KTU PCBML507 – Module Experiment 2**, *detection of events and waves* refers to the systematic application of digital signal processing (DSP) techniques — including bandpass filtering, spectral analysis, thresholding, and peak-detection algorithms — to automatically identify, segment, and classify the constituent frequency bands and transient features embedded in a multi-channel EEG recording. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Distinction Between Waves and Events** > - **Waves**: Sustained rhythmic oscillations characterized by a *narrow frequency band* and quasi-stationary statistics over hundreds of milliseconds to seconds. > - **Events**: Transient, time-localized morphologies (spikes, sharp waves, spindles, K-complexes) with duration typically **20–200 ms** for epileptiform transients and **0.5–2 s** for sleep-related graphoelements. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Orchestra Inside Your Skull" Imagine the brain as a **symphony orchestra playing inside a concert hall**: | Brain Concept | Orchestra Analogy | |---|---| | Cortical neurons | Individual musicians | | Synchronized neural populations | Instrument sections (strings, brass) | | EEG waves (Delta, Theta, Alpha, Beta) | Sustained musical notes / chords | | EEG events (spikes, spindles) | Sudden drum hits, cymbal crashes | | EEG electrode | Microphone hanging from the ceiling | | Noise (EMG, EOG, 50 Hz line) | Background chatter of the audience | Just as a sound engineer uses **filters** (equalizers) to isolate the violin section from the drums, an EEG signal processor uses **bandpass filters** to separate Delta from Alpha activity. *Events* are like sudden percussive beats in the music — short, sharp, and easy to spot once you know what to listen for. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Adult EEG Amplitude Range**: **10–100 µV** (compared to ECG at ~1 mV — EEG is roughly **10× smaller**, making detection challenging in the presence of artifacts). --- ## 1.3 The Five Canonical EEG Rhythms The International Federation of Clinical Neurophysiology (IFCN) and KTU-recommended standard classifications define five primary frequency bands: | Wave Band | Frequency Range (Hz) | Typical Amplitude (µV) | Physiological State / Source | |---|---|---|---| | **Delta (δ)** | **0.5 – 4** | 20 – 200 | Deep (slow-wave) sleep; infants | | **Theta (θ)** | **4 – 8** | < 20 | Drowsiness, meditation, childhood | | **Alpha (α)** | **8 – 13** | 20 – 60 | Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed (posterior dominance) | | **Beta (β)** | **13 – 30** | < 20 | Active thinking, alert focus, anxiety | | **Gamma (γ)** | **> 30** | Very low | Higher cognition, perception binding | --- ## 1.4 Major EEG Transients (Events) | Event | Duration | Morphology | Clinical Context | |---|---|---|---| | **Spike** | **20 – 70 ms** | Sharply contoured, pointed peak | Epileptiform discharges | | **Sharp wave** | **70 – 200 ms** | Pointed but less sharp than spike | Epileptiform, also benign variants | | **Sleep spindle** | **0.5 – 2 s** | Waxing-waning 12–14 Hz burst | Stage 2 NREM sleep | | **K-complex** | **≥ 0.5 s** | Sharp negative then positive deflection | Stage 2 NREM, arousal response | | **Vertex sharp wave** | < 200 ms | Sharply peaked at Cz | Stage 1 NREM sleep | | **Lambda wave** | 100 – 300 ms | Sharp occipital positivity | Visual scanning | > [!IMPORTANT] > For KTU lab evaluation purposes, the **most commonly tested detection problems** involve: (1) Delta/Alpha rhythm isolation via FFT, (2) spike detection via amplitude/derivative thresholding, and (3) sleep spindle detection via bandpass filtering + envelope detection. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Pipeline for EEG Event/Wave Detection The standard KTU-recommended processing chain consists of **five sequential stages**. Each stage addresses a specific noise source or feature isolation requirement. ### Stage 1 — Preprocessing & Artifact Removal - **DC offset removal** (subtract channel mean) - **Powerline notch filter** at **50 Hz** (India) or **60 Hz** with Q-factor ≈ 30 - **High-pass filter** at **0.5 Hz** to eliminate baseline drift - **Low-pass anti-alias** before downsampling (cut-off = 0.8 × Nyquist) ### Stage 2 — Bandpass Filtering for Wave Isolation A 4th-order **Butterworth** bandpass filter is the KTU default because of its maximally flat passband (no ripple). For wave *w* with center frequency *f₀* and bandwidth *BW*: $$f_{\text{low}} = f_0 - \frac{BW}{2}, \quad f_{\text{high}} = f_0 + \frac{BW}{2}$$ ### Stage 3 — Feature Extraction Three feature families are used: 1. **Time-domain features**: peak amplitude, zero-crossing rate, line-length, root-mean-square (RMS) energy 2. **Frequency-domain features**: band power via periodogram or Welch's method, spectral edge frequency 3. **Time-frequency features**: short-time Fourier transform (STFT), continuous wavelet transform (CWT) using Morlet wavelet ### Stage 4 — Detection (Thresholding) - **Amplitude threshold**: candidate if amplitude exceeds $\mu + k\sigma$ (where $\mu$ = mean, $\sigma$ = SD of filtered signal in a sliding window, typically $k = 3$ to $5$) - **Duration constraint**: candidate must persist for a minimum number of samples matching the target event duration - **Sliding window**: typically 1–2 s with 50% overlap ### Stage 5 — Classification / Post-processing - Rule-based or machine-learning classifier - False-positive rejection using minimum inter-event interval (refractory period, typically 200 ms for spikes) --- ## 2.2 Why These Steps? — The Underlying "Why" | Step | Purpose | Engineering Justification | |---|---|---| | Notch filter | Removes 50 Hz mains interference | EEG amplitude (~50 µV) is comparable to coupled line noise | | Bandpass filter | Isolates frequency band of interest | SNR improvement scales with $\sqrt{BW_{\text{in}}/BW_{\text{out}}}$ | | Thresholding | Converts continuous signal to binary decisions | Optimal under Gaussian noise (Neyman-Pearson criterion) | | Duration constraint | Distinguishes spikes from muscle artifact | Spikes have stereotyped duration; EMG is broadband and random | | Refractory period | Prevents double-counting of a single event | A single spike may trigger multiple threshold crossings | --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every Part B question on this topic.** | # | Equation | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x^2[n]$ | Average signal power (time-domain) | µV² | | 2 | $\text{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x^2[n]}$ | Root-mean-square amplitude | µV | | 3 | $S_{xx}[k] = \frac{1}{N}\left\vert X[k]\right^2$ | Periodogram power spectral density | µV²/Hz | | 4 | $\hat{S}_{xx}^{\text{Welch}}(f) = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{i=1}^{K} S_{xx}^{(i)}(f)$ | Welch's averaged periodogram (K segments, Hanning window) | µV²/Hz | | 5 | $\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N}$ | Frequency resolution of an N-point FFT | Hz | | 6 | $f_{\text{Nyquist}} = \frac{f_s}{2}$ | Maximum representable frequency | Hz | | 7 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \text{SNR}_{\text{in}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{BW_{\text{in}}}{BW_{\text{out}}}}$ | SNR gain from bandpass filtering | dimensionless | | 8 | $\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Detection sensitivity (true positive rate) | 0 to 1 | | 9 | $\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | True negative rate | 0 to 1 | | 10 | $\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Overall classifier accuracy | 0 to 1 | | 11 | $L = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N-1}\vert x[n+1] - x[n]\vert$ | Line-length feature (spike detector) | µV/sample | | 12 | $T = \mu_w + k\sigma_w$ | Adaptive threshold from local window | µV | | 13 | $\Delta t_{\text{refrac}} \geq 200$ ms | Minimum inter-spike interval | ms | | 14 | $\text{CWT}_\psi(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}}\int x(t)\,\psi^*\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)dt$ | Continuous wavelet transform (Morlet) | depends on ψ | | 15 | $f_a = \frac{f_c}{a\cdot \Delta t}$ | Pseudo-frequency at scale *a* | Hz | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering EEG event/wave detection forms the algorithmic backbone of: 1. **Clinical epilepsy monitoring** — automated spike detection reduces neurologist review time by ~70% in long-term EEG recordings. 2. **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI)** — motor imagery BCI decodes Alpha/Beta event-related desynchronization (ERD). 3. **Sleep staging systems** — automated hypnogram generation per Rechtschaffen & Kales (R&K) or AASM standards depends on spindle/K-complex detection. 4. **Neuropharmacological drug trials** — quantitative EEG (qEEG) band-power changes are used as biomarkers. 5. **ICU neurological monitoring** — continuous EEG (cEEG) with automated detection of seizures, spreading depolarizations, and burst-suppression patterns. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Periodogram-Based Band Power Estimation The band power of an EEG signal in frequency band $[f_1, f_2]$ is a foundational quantity in wave detection. We derive it rigorously. ### Step 1 — Acquire Windowed Segment Take a windowed EEG segment $x_w[n] = x[n]\cdot w[n]$, where $w[n]$ is typically a Hanning window of length $N$, applied to reduce spectral leakage. The Hanning window is defined as: $$w[n] = 0.5 - 0.5\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right), \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$ ### Step 2 — Compute the Discrete Fourier Transform Apply the DFT (efficiently via FFT): $$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x_w[n]\,e^{-j2\pi kn/N}, \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$ ### Step 3 — Form the Periodogram (Power Spectral Density Estimate) The periodogram is the squared magnitude normalized by $N$: $$S_{xx}[k] = \frac{1}{N}\left\vert X[k]\right^2$$ > This is a one-sided PSD if we later multiply the positive frequencies (except DC and Nyquist) by 2. ### Step 4 — Map Bin Index to Physical Frequency The frequency corresponding to FFT bin $k$ is: $$f_k = \frac{k \cdot f_s}{N}$$ The frequency spacing between adjacent bins is: $$\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N}$$ ### Step 5 — Sum PSD Over the Frequency Band of Interest Band power in $[f_1, f_2]$ is obtained by summing all bins whose frequencies fall within the band: $$P_{\text{band}} = \sum_{k\,:\,f_1 \leq f_k \leq f_2} S_{xx}[k] \cdot \Delta f$$ Multiplying by $\Delta f$ is essential because $S_{xx}[k]$ has units of µV²/Hz, and summation without it yields µV²·(dimensionless) only if interpreted carefully — for the standard periodogram definition above, $P_{\text{band}}$ has units µV². ### Step 6 — Welch's Averaging for Variance Reduction A single periodogram has variance of order $\mathcal{O}(P^2)$ (large). Welch's method splits the signal into $K$ overlapping segments (50% overlap, Hanning window each), computes the periodogram of each, then averages: $$\hat{S}_{xx}^{\text{Welch}}(f) = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{i=1}^{K} S_{xx}^{(i)}(f)$$ > This reduces variance by factor $K$ at the cost of frequency resolution (each segment is shorter: $N_{\text{seg}} = N/K_{\text{overlap}}$). --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Adaptive Amplitude Threshold for Spike Detection Let $x[n]$ be an EEG segment bandpass-filtered to the **1–70 Hz** spike-relevant band. We seek a threshold that adapts to local baseline. ### Step 1 — Estimate Local Mean and Standard Deviation In a sliding window of duration $W$ (typically **1–3 seconds**): $$\mu_w[n] = \frac{1}{W}\sum_{m=n-W/2}^{n+W/2} x[m]$$ $$\sigma_w[n] = \sqrt{\frac{1}{W}\sum_{m=n-W/2}^{n+W/2}(x[m] - \mu_w[n])^2}$$ ### Step 2 — Define Adaptive Threshold A candidate sample $x[n]$ is flagged as a spike-onset if: $$\left\vert x[n] - \mu_w[n]\right\vert > T[n] = \mu_w[n] + k\cdot\sigma_w[n]$$ Typical values: **k = 3 to 5**. Larger *k* → fewer false positives, more false negatives. ### Step 3 — Apply Duration Constraint A spike must have a peak-to-trough duration between **20 ms** and **70 ms**. Convert to samples: $$N_{\min} = \frac{20 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot f_s}{1} = 0.02\,f_s$$ $$N_{\max} = \frac{70 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot f_s}{1} = 0.07\,f_s$$ For $f_s = 256$ Hz: $N_{\min} \approx 5$ samples, $N_{\max} \approx 18$ samples. ### Step 4 — Refractory Period After detecting a spike at sample $n_0$, suppress all further detections in the window: $$n_{\text{refrac}} = n_0 + 0.2 \cdot f_s$$ For $f_s = 256$ Hz: $n_{\text{refrac}} = n_0 + 51$ samples. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Alpha Band Power **Problem:** An EEG channel is sampled at $f_s = 256$ Hz. A 4-second epoch ($N = 1024$ samples) is analyzed. The dominant spectral peak occurs at FFT bin $k = 16$. Compute the corresponding physical frequency. ### Solution $$f_k = \frac{k \cdot f_s}{N} = \frac{16 \cdot 256}{1024} = \frac{4096}{1024} = 4 \text{ Hz}$$ Wait — 4 Hz is **Theta**, not Alpha. Re-examine: bin $k = 40$ would yield: $$f_{40} = \frac{40 \cdot 256}{1024} = 10 \text{ Hz}$$ which lies in the **Alpha band (8–13 Hz)**. ✓ ### Frequency Resolution $$\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N} = \frac{256}{1024} = 0.25 \text{ Hz}$$ Excellent resolution — well within KTU expectations. ### Effective Bandwidth of Alpha Region For Alpha (8–13 Hz), the number of FFT bins: $$N_{\text{alpha}} = \frac{f_{\text{high}} - f_{\text{low}}}{\Delta f} = \frac{13 - 8}{0.25} = 20 \text{ bins}$$ If each bin in this range has averaged PSD $\bar{S} = 15\ \mu\text{V}^2/\text{Hz}$, the band power is: $$P_{\alpha} = \bar{S} \cdot (f_{\text{high}} - f_{\text{low}}) = 15 \cdot 5 = 75\ \mu\text{V}^2$$ The RMS amplitude in the Alpha band: $$\text{RMS}_\alpha = \sqrt{P_\alpha} = \sqrt{75} \approx 8.66\ \mu\text{V}$$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Detector | Target | Recommended Method | Threshold / Parameter | Typical Sensitivity | |---|---|---|---| | Delta rhythm | Welch PSD + band integration | 0.5–4 Hz | 92% (sleep staging) | | Alpha rhythm | Bandpass 8–13 Hz + RMS | RMS > 10 µV | 88% (eyes-closed detection) | | Beta rhythm | Bandpass 13–30 Hz + envelope | Envelope > 7 µV | 85% | | Sleep spindle | Bandpass 12–14 Hz + RMS over 0.5 s | RMS > 8 µV sustained 0.5–2 s | 78% | | K-complex | Morphological detector (negative peak + positive peak) | Amplitude > 75 µV, duration > 0.5 s | 70% | | Spike | Adaptive threshold (k=3–5) | abs(x) > µ + k·σ | 80–90% | | Sharp wave | Same as spike + duration 70–200 ms | abs(x) > µ + k·σ | 75% | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Numerical Insight**: SNR improvement from bandpass filtering equals $\sqrt{BW_{\text{in}}/BW_{\text{out}}}$. Filtering raw EEG (BW = 0.5–70 Hz = 69.5 Hz) to extract Alpha (BW = 5 Hz) yields SNR gain of $\sqrt{69.5/5} = \sqrt{13.9} \approx 3.73$ — almost **+11.4 dB**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — End-to-End EEG Event/Wave Detection Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw EEG Signal
Multi-channel
f sub s = 256 Hz] --> B[Preprocessing
DC removal
Notch 50 Hz] B --> C[Bandpass Filter
0.5 to 70 Hz
4th order Butterworth] C --> D{Detection Stage
Selection} D --> E1[Wave Detection
Path] D --> E2[Event Detection
Path] E1 --> F1[Band Selection
Delta Theta Alpha Beta Gamma] F1 --> G1[Welch PSD
Hanning window
50 percent overlap] G1 --> H1[Band Power
Integration] H1 --> I1[Classification
Threshold on
absolute power] E2 --> F2[Adaptive Thresholding
k equals 3 to 5 sigma] F2 --> G2[Duration Check
20 to 200 ms] G2 --> H2[Refractory Period
200 ms] H2 --> I2[Event List
Onset Peak Offset] I1 --> J[Output
Visualization and Report] I2 --> J ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation Guide**: The flowchart shows two parallel detection tracks. The *Wave Detection Path* (left branch) uses spectral methods suited for sustained oscillations. The *Event Detection Path* (right branch) uses time-domain thresholding suited for transients. --- ## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Spike Detection Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Input A[EEG Channel x of n] end subgraph Filter Bank B[BPF 1 to 70 Hz
Butterworth order 4] end subgraph Feature Engine C[Squaring
x squared of n] D[Moving Average
Window W = 100 ms] E[Line Length
L of n] end subgraph Decision Unit F[Threshold Comparator
T = mu + k sigma] G[Duration Logic
20 to 70 ms] H[Refractory Logic
200 ms] end subgraph Output I[Spike Onset Time
Amplitude and Duration] end A --> B B --> C B --> E C --> D D --> F E --> F F --> G G --> H H --> I ``` --- ## 4.3 Frequency-Domain Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Compute FFT
N = 1024] --> B[Estimate PSD
Welch method] B --> C{Band Classification} C --> D1[0.5 to 4 Hz
Delta] C --> D2[4 to 8 Hz
Theta] C --> D3[8 to 13 Hz
Alpha] C --> D4[13 to 30 Hz
Beta] C --> D5[above 30 Hz
Gamma] D1 --> E[Sum PSD bins
in band] D2 --> E D3 --> E D4 --> E D5 --> E E --> F[Compare to
normative thresholds] F --> G[Classify
Brain State] ``` --- ## 4.4 Time-Frequency View — Wavelet-Based Spike Localization ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw EEG] --> B[CWT
Morlet wavelet] B --> C[Scalogram
Time vs Scale] C --> D[Detect Ridges
Local maxima
across scales] D --> E[Extract Peak
Frequency and Time] E --> F[Spike Localization
Time and Frequency
Coefficients] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]` **Differentiate between EEG waves and EEG events with two examples each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | EEG Waves | EEG Events | |---|---|---| | Duration | Sustained (hundreds of ms to seconds) | Transient (20–200 ms for epileptiform) | | Frequency | Quasi-stationary, narrowband | Broadband, non-stationary | | Examples | Delta (0.5–4 Hz), Alpha (8–13 Hz) | Spike, Sharp wave | | Detection | Spectral (FFT/Welch) | Time-domain threshold / morphological | *(1 mark each for definition + example distinction; 1 mark for detection method contrast.)* --- ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]` **List the five standard EEG frequency bands with their frequency ranges and one physiological correlate each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Delta (δ): 0.5–4 Hz** — Deep sleep 2. **Theta (θ): 4–8 Hz** — Drowsiness / meditation 3. **Alpha (α): 8–13 Hz** — Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed 4. **Beta (β): 13–30 Hz** — Active thinking / alertness 5. **Gamma (γ): > 30 Hz** — Higher cognitive binding *(½ mark per band — frequency range + correlate.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > KTU 2024 Scheme mandates an *internal choice* in Part B. **Both options** are provided below. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — KTU 2024 Scheme]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **end-to-end pipeline for automated EEG spike detection** in a long-term epilepsy monitoring recording. Mention the role of each block. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An EEG segment of 4 seconds is sampled at $f_s = 256$ Hz and its FFT is computed using $N = 1024$ points. The dominant peak occurs at FFT bin $k = 32$. - (i) Compute the corresponding physical frequency and identify the EEG band. - (ii) If the Welch-averaged PSD in the **8–13 Hz Alpha band** equals $\bar{S} = 12\ \mu\text{V}^2/\text{Hz}$, compute the Alpha band power and its RMS amplitude. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Pipeline Description (7 marks)** The spike detection pipeline consists of **five sequential blocks**: 1. **Acquisition & Preprocessing (1 mark)** - Multi-channel EEG sampled at 256–512 Hz - DC offset removal (subtract mean) - 50 Hz notch filter (Q ≈ 30) - Bandpass filter 0.5–70 Hz, 4th-order Butterworth 2. **Channel Selection / Referencing (1 mark)** - Common Average Reference (CAR) to suppress common-mode noise - Bipolar montages for focal spike enhancement 3. **Feature Extraction (2 marks)** - Squaring + moving-average envelope (window ≈ 100 ms) → energy feature -
