Line-length $L[n] = \frac{1}{W}\sum_{m=n-W}^{n+W} \vert x[m+1] - x[m]\vert$ - Absolute amplitude $\vert x[n]\vert$ 4. **Adaptive Thresholding (2 marks)** - In sliding window $W$ (1–3 s): estimate $\mu_w$, $\sigma_w$ - Threshold $T = \mu_w + k\sigma_w$ with $k = 3$ to $5$ - Flag crossings as candidate spikes 5. **Post-processing / Classification (1 mark)** - Duration check (20–70 ms) - Refractory period ≥ 200 ms - Optional: ANN/SVM classifier to reject false positives --- **Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)** **(i) Frequency and band identification (3 marks)** $$\begin{aligned} f_k &= \frac{k \cdot f_s}{N} = \frac{32 \cdot 256}{1024} \\[4pt] f_k &= \frac{8192}{1024} = 8 \text{ Hz} \end{aligned}$$ Since 8 Hz falls within the **Alpha band (8–13 Hz)**, the dominant rhythm is Alpha — typical of a relaxed, eyes-closed subject. *(2 marks for computation, 1 mark for band identification.)* **(ii) Alpha band power and RMS (4 marks)** $$\begin{aligned} P_{\alpha} &= \bar{S} \cdot (f_{\text{high}} - f_{\text{low}}) \\[4pt] P_{\alpha} &= 12\ \mu\text{V}^2/\text{Hz} \cdot (13 - 8)\ \text{Hz} \\[4pt] P_{\alpha} &= 60\ \mu\text{V}^2 \end{aligned}$$ $$\text{RMS}_\alpha = \sqrt{P_\alpha} = \sqrt{60} \approx 7.746\ \mu\text{V}$$ *(2 marks each for power and RMS, including units.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — KTU 2024 Scheme — Alternative Choice]` **(a)** Explain the **Welch method of power spectral density estimation** for EEG signals. State its advantages over the standard periodogram and derive the expression for frequency resolution. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For spike detection in an EEG channel sampled at $f_s = 200$ Hz, an adaptive threshold is set as $T = \mu_w + 4\sigma_w$, where $\mu_w$ and $\sigma_w$ are computed in a sliding window of duration 1 s. - (i) Express the minimum and maximum spike duration in terms of samples. - (ii) Compute the refractory period in samples and justify why it is required. - (iii) If $\mu_w = 5\ \mu\text{V}$ and $\sigma_w = 3\ \mu\text{V}$, compute the detection threshold $T$. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Welch Method (7 marks)** 1. **Standard Periodogram Limitation (2 marks)** - Single DFT over entire signal → variance of order $\mathcal{O}(P^2)$, does not decrease with $N$. - High spectral leakage due to windowing truncation. 2. **Welch Procedure (3 marks)** - Segment signal into $K$ overlapping windows (typically 50% overlap), each of length $L$. - Apply Hanning window to each segment. - Compute periodogram of each segment: $S_{xx}^{(i)}[k] = \frac{1}{L}\left\vert X_i[k]\right^2$ - Average across segments: $$\hat{S}_{xx}^{\text{Welch}}(f) = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{i=1}^{K} S_{xx}^{(i)}(f)$$ - Variance reduced by factor $K$ compared to single periodogram. 3. **Frequency Resolution (2 marks)** $$\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{L}$$ where $L$ is the segment length. Larger $L$ → better resolution but fewer segments $K$, increasing variance. Trade-off controlled by user. --- **Part (b) — Spike Detection Numerical (7 marks)** **(i) Spike duration in samples (2 marks)** $$N_{\min} = \frac{20 \times 10^{-3} \cdot f_s}{1} = 0.020 \cdot 200 = 4 \text{ samples}$$ $$N_{\max} = \frac{70 \times 10^{-3} \cdot f_s}{1} = 0.070 \cdot 200 = 14 \text{ samples}$$ **(ii) Refractory period (3 marks)** $$n_{\text{refrac}} = \frac{200 \times 10^{-3} \cdot f_s}{1} = 0.200 \cdot 200 = 40 \text{ samples}$$ *Justification*: A single suprathreshold spike can cross the threshold multiple times due to ringing of the filter and oscillatory tail. Without a refractory period, one physiological spike is counted as multiple events, falsely inflating the spike rate. The 200 ms window corresponds to typical maximum neuronal refractory time. *(1 mark computation + 2 marks justification.)* **(iii) Threshold value (2 marks)** $$\begin{aligned} T &= \mu_w + 4\sigma_w = 5 + 4 \cdot 3 \\[4pt] T &= 5 + 12 = 17\ \mu\text{V} \end{aligned}$$ Any sample whose absolute amplitude exceeds 17 µV (relative to the local mean) is flagged as a candidate spike onset. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Forgetting units**: Always state µV² for power, Hz for frequency, ms for duration. Marks are deducted if units are omitted. > 2. **Confusing PSD units**: $S_{xx}$ is in µV²/Hz, *not* µV². Band power requires multiplying by bandwidth. > 3. **Misidentifying Alpha vs Theta boundary**: Alpha starts at **8 Hz**, not 10 Hz. Many students lose marks here. > 4. **Periodogram vs Welch**: If asked "why Welch", the *variance reduction* argument (factor $K$) is mandatory — not "because it is smoother". > 5. **Spike duration**: Standard IFCN defines **spike = 20–70 ms**, **sharp wave = 70–200 ms**. Reversing these definitions costs 1 mark. > 6. **Refractory period**: Must be stated in *both* ms and samples (sample value depends on $f_s$). > 7. **Nyquist**: If $f_s = 256$ Hz, max representable frequency is **128 Hz**, not 256 Hz. Never write $f_{\text{max}} = f_s$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Must memorize before exam** - ☐ **5 EEG bands**: Delta (0.5–4), Theta (4–8), Alpha (8–13), Beta (13–30), Gamma (>30) Hz - ☐ **EEG amplitude range**: **10–100 µV** (≈ 10× smaller than ECG) - ☐ **Sampling rate**: Minimum **256 Hz** for clinical EEG; **512 Hz** for spike detection - ☐ **Spike duration**: **20–70 ms**; Sharp wave: **70–200 ms** (IFCN standard) - ☐ **Sleep spindle**: 12–14 Hz, **0.5–2 s** duration, Stage 2 NREM - ☐ **K-complex**: Sharp biphasic wave, **≥ 0.5 s**, NREM Stage 2 - ☐ **Refractory period**: ≥ **200 ms** for spike detection - ☐ **Nyquist frequency**: $f_s / 2$ - ☐ **Frequency resolution**: $\Delta f = f_s / N$ - ☐ **Welch PSD**: averaged periodogram with **Hanning window + 50% overlap** - ☐ **SNR gain from bandpass**: $\sqrt{BW_{\text{in}} / BW_{\text{out}}}$ - ☐ **Adaptive threshold formula**: $T = \mu_w + k\sigma_w$, $k = 3$ to $5$ - ☐ **Detection metrics**: Sensitivity $= TP/(TP+FN)$, Specificity $= TN/(TN+FP)$, Accuracy $= (TP+TN)/\text{Total}$ - ☐ **Line-length feature**: $L = \frac{1}{N}\sum \vert x[n+1] - x[n]\vert$ — sensitive to sharp deflections - ☐ **50 Hz notch filter**: mandatory in India due to powerline interference - ☐ **Butterworth order 4** is the default for EEG bandpass filtering (maximally flat passband) - ☐ **Welch vs Periodogram**: Welch variance $\downarrow$ by factor $K$; frequency resolution $\downarrow$ by factor $K$ - ☐ **Time-frequency method for non-stationary events**: Continuous Wavelet Transform (Morlet wavelet) - ☐ **CWT pseudo-frequency formula**: $f_a = f_c / (a \cdot \Delta t)$ - ☐ **Absolute vs relative band power**: Absolute = µV²; Relative = $P_{\text{band}}/P_{\text{total}}$ (dimensionless) - ☐ **Common montages**: Common Average Reference (CAR), bipolar, referential (linked-ears)

topics.title: Detection of events and heart rate measurement from PPG and ECG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML507 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Detection of events and heart rate measurement from PPG and ECG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: DETECTION OF EVENTS AND HEART RATE MEASUREMENT FROM PPG AND ECG

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Electrocardiogram (ECG)

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
The **Electrocardiogram (ECG)** is a bioelectric signal that records the electrical activity of the heart over time, captured using surface electrodes placed on the body. It represents the sum of the action potentials generated by the cardiac muscle cells (myocytes) during each cardiac cycle, sampled typically at **250 Hz to 1000 Hz** in clinical and research settings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard ECG Waveform Components (PQRST Complex):**
> - **P Wave**: Atrial depolarization (atria contract)
> - **QRS Complex**: Ventricular depolarization (ventricles contract) — the most prominent feature, used for HR detection
> - **T Wave**: Ventricular repolarization (ventricles relax)
> - **U Wave** (sometimes): Repolarization of Purkinje fibers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **R-peak** is the tallest, sharpest positive deflection in the QRS complex and is the **primary fiducial point** for heart rate calculation, because it is the most reliably detectable event in the ECG.

### 1.2 Photoplethysmogram (PPG)

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
The **Photoplethysmogram (PPG)** is an optical signal obtained by illuminating the skin (typically at a fingertip, earlobe, or wrist) with a light source and measuring the intensity of reflected or transmitted light. The signal correlates with volumetric changes in the microvascular bed of tissue caused by the cardiac cycle, sampled typically at **100 Hz to 500 Hz**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard PPG Waveform Components:**
> - **Systolic Peak (S-peak)**: Maximum amplitude — corresponds to the peak blood volume during ventricular systole
> - **Dicrotic Notch (DN)**: Small downward deflection — caused by the closure of the aortic valve
> - **Diastolic Peak (D-peak)**: Secondary peak — corresponds to the reflected pressure wave from the periphery
> - **Pulse Interval**: Time between two consecutive systolic peaks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **systolic peak** of the PPG is the **primary fiducial point** used to compute the **Pulse Rate (PR)**, which is generally equivalent to the Heart Rate (HR) in healthy individuals at rest.

### 1.3 Heart Rate (HR)

**Formal Definition:**
**Heart Rate (HR)** is defined as the number of cardiac cycles (heartbeats) per unit time, conventionally expressed in **Beats Per Minute (BPM)**.

$$\text{HR (BPM)} = \frac{60}{T_{\text{cycle}}}$$

where $T_{\text{cycle}}$ is the duration of one cardiac cycle in **seconds**, measured as the time interval between two consecutive R-peaks (for ECG) or two consecutive systolic peaks (for PPG).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Normal Adult Resting HR**: **60 – 100 BPM**
> - **Bradycardia**: HR < 60 BPM
> - **Tachycardia**: HR > 100 BPM
> - **Athletes (resting)**: May be 40 – 60 BPM (physiological)

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogies (Intuitive Understanding)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — The Heart as a Drum:**
> Imagine the heart as a drummer tapping a steady beat inside a closed concert hall. The **ECG** is like recording the *electrical command* sent to the drummer (the conductor's baton signals), while the **PPG** is like recording the *sound waves* the drumming produces at the hall's walls. Both can be used to count beats, but they pick up the rhythm through completely different physical means (electrical vs. optical).

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Traffic on a Bridge:**
> Think of an artery as a single-lane bridge. The **ECG R-peak** is the moment the traffic light turns green (electrical permission to squeeze blood out), and the **PPG systolic peak** is the moment the bridge is most crowded with cars (maximum blood volume). By counting either the green flashes or the bridge-full moments per minute, you can determine the heart rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — R-peak as the Lighthouse Flash:**
> The R-peak in an ECG is like a lighthouse flash on a dark coastline. It is brief, sharp, and easy to spot from far away. Even if waves (noise, baseline wander) crash on the shore, an observer with a steady gaze can count each flash reliably and calculate the time between flashes to find the rate of rotation (heart rate).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Event Detection

The detection of cardiac events (R-peaks in ECG, systolic peaks in PPG) is a classical **signal processing pattern recognition** problem. The complete pipeline consists of four major stages:

1. **Signal Acquisition** — Capture the raw biosignal using appropriate hardware.
2. **Pre-processing** — Remove noise, baseline wander, and motion artifacts.
3. **Feature Extraction / Peak Detection** — Locate the fiducial points (R-peaks or S-peaks).
4. **Post-processing & HR Computation** — Calculate intervals and convert to BPM.

### 2.2 ECG Pre-processing Theory

ECG signals are corrupted by several noise sources that must be addressed before peak detection:

- **Powerline Interference**: 50/60 Hz sinusoidal noise → removed by **notch filter**
- **Baseline Wander**: Low-frequency drift (0 – 0.5 Hz) due to respiration → removed by **high-pass filter** or **polynomial fitting**
- **Muscle (EMG) Noise**: High-frequency random noise → removed by **low-pass filter**
- **Combined**: A **bandpass filter (0.5 Hz – 40 Hz)** is the standard preprocessing choice

### 2.3 Pan-Tompkins Algorithm (Gold-Standard for QRS Detection)

The **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** (1985) is the de-facto benchmark for real-time R-peak detection. It consists of five stages:

| Stage | Operation | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1. Bandpass Filtering** | Cascade of LPF (cutoff 11 Hz) + HPF (cutoff 5 Hz) | Isolate QRS energy (5 – 15 Hz band) |
| **2. Derivative** | Five-point derivative operator: $y[n] = (1/8)(2x[n+1]+x[n]-x[n-1]-2x[n-2])$ | Suppress P and T waves; emphasize QRS slope |
| **3. Squaring** | $y[n] = (x[n])^2$ | Make all values positive; emphasize large differences |
| **4. Moving Window Integration** | $y[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[n-k]$, where $N = 0.15 \cdot f_s$ | Smooth and produce a single pulse per QRS |
| **5. Adaptive Thresholding** | Dual thresholds updated by peak amplitude | Robust detection across amplitude variations |

### 2.4 PPG Pre-processing Theory

PPG signals are smoother than ECG but suffer from:

- **Motion Artifacts**: Large irregular spikes
- **Baseline Wander**: From subject movement and respiration
- **Ambient Light Interference**: DC and high-frequency components

A **bandpass filter (0.5 Hz – 8 Hz)** is typically applied, since the heart rate range translates to:

- 40 BPM → 0.67 Hz
- 200 BPM → 3.33 Hz

### 2.5 Peak Detection Methods (General)

| Method | Principle | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Fixed Threshold** | Detect when signal crosses a hardcoded value | Simple, requires amplitude stability |
| **Adaptive Threshold** | Threshold follows running mean + k·std | Robust to amplitude drift |
| **First Derivative Zero-Crossing** | Locate zero-crossings of $dy/dt$ | Effective for sharp QRS |
| **Pan-Tompkins** | Multi-stage QRS-specific pipeline | Gold standard for ECG |
| **`findpeaks` (MATLAB)** | Local maxima with min-peak-distance constraint | Universal; works on both ECG and PPG |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimum Peak Distance Constraint**: A physiological safety constraint is applied so that detected peaks are at least **0.3 s (200 BPM maximum)** apart. This prevents the algorithm from double-counting within a single QRS complex.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Parameter | Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\text{HR} = \dfrac{60}{T_{\text{RR}}}$ | $T_{\text{RR}}$ = R-R interval | seconds, BPM | ECG-based HR |
| 2 | $\text{HR} = \dfrac{60}{T_{\text{PP}}}$ | $T_{\text{PP}}$ = PPG peak-to-peak interval | seconds, BPM | PPG-based HR |
| 3 | $\text{HR} = \dfrac{f_s \times 60}{N_{\text{samples}}}$ | $f_s$ = sampling frequency, $N_{\text{samples}}$ = samples between peaks | Hz, BPM | Discrete-time form |
| 4 | $T_{\text{RR}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{peak}_2} - n_{\text{peak}_1}}{f_s}$ | $n_{\text{peak}}$ = sample index of R-peak | seconds | Used after peak detection |
| 5 | $\overline{\text{HR}} = \dfrac{60}{\overline{T_{\text{RR}}}}$ | Mean over multiple cycles | BPM | Averaging improves accuracy |
| 6 | Pan-Tompkins BPF | $f_{\text{pass}} = 5 - 15$ Hz | Hz | QRS energy band |
| 7 | Pan-Tompkins MA window | $N_{\text{MA}} = 0.15 \cdot f_s$ | samples | ≈ 150 ms integration |
| 8 | Normal Resting HR | $60 \leq \text{HR} \leq 100$ | BPM | Clinical reference |
| 9 | Bradycardia threshold | $\text{HR} < 60$ | BPM | Pathological or athletic |
| 10 | Tachycardia threshold | $\text{HR} > 100$ | BPM | Pathological or exertion |
| 11 | $f_{\text{HR}} = \dfrac{\text{HR}}{60}$ | Convert BPM to Hz | Hz | Useful for filter design |
| 12 | SDNN (HRV metric) | $\sigma_{T_{\text{RR}}}$ | ms | Standard deviation of RR intervals |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the backbone of every **wearable fitness tracker** (Fitbit, Apple Watch), **patient monitor** in ICUs, **ambulatory Holter recorders**, **pulse oximeters**, and **AI-based cardiac diagnostic systems**. Accurate R-peak detection is the first step in computing **Heart Rate Variability (HRV)**, which is a critical biomarker for stress, autonomic nervous system health, and arrhythmia detection.

## 2.7 Practical MATLAB Implementation Concept

In the KTU lab, MATLAB's Signal Processing Toolbox is used. The key functions are:

- `load` / `readtable` — Load the recorded `.mat` or `.csv` file
- `detrend` — Remove baseline wander (DC and slow trends)
- `bandpass` (R2020b+) or `butter` + `filtfilt` — Apply bandpass filter
- `findpeaks` — Detect R-peaks or systolic peaks
- `diff` — Compute the inter-peak intervals
- `mean` — Average to obtain final HR
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heart Rate from RR Interval (ECG)

The derivation proceeds from the fundamental definition of frequency applied to a periodic cardiac signal.

**Step 1 — Define the cardiac cycle period.**

The cardiac cycle period $T_{\text{RR}}$ is the time elapsed between two consecutive R-peaks. If the sample index of the $k$-th R-peak is $n_k$, then:

$$T_{\text{RR}}^{(k)} = \frac{n_{k+1} - n_k}{f_s}$$

where $f_s$ is the sampling frequency in Hz. The numerator $n_{k+1} - n_k$ is the number of samples between the two peaks, and dividing by $f_s$ converts sample count to time in seconds.

**Step 2 — Define the instantaneous heart rate.**

By the standard definition of a periodic signal's rate, the instantaneous heart rate is the reciprocal of the period:

$$f_{\text{HR}}^{(k)} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{RR}}^{(k)}}$$

This $f_{\text{HR}}$ is in units of Hz (cycles per second). Since 1 Hz = 60 cycles per minute, we convert:

$$\text{HR}^{(k)} = 60 \times f_{\text{HR}}^{(k)} = \frac{60}{T_{\text{RR}}^{(k)}}$$

Substituting the expression for $T_{\text{RR}}^{(k)}$:

$$\text{HR}^{(k)} = \frac{60 \cdot f_s}{n_{k+1} - n_k}$$

**Step 3 — Average over multiple cycles for robustness.**

To reduce the effect of beat-to-beat variability and noise, the mean HR is computed over $M$ consecutive cycles:

$$\overline{\text{HR}} = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{k=1}^{M} \text{HR}^{(k)} = \frac{60}{M} \sum_{k=1}^{M} \frac{f_s}{n_{k+1} - n_k}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternative (and more stable) form:** It is mathematically equivalent — and numerically more stable — to first average the $T_{\text{RR}}$ values, then take the reciprocal:
> $$\overline{\text{HR}} = \frac{60}{\overline{T_{\text{RR}}}} = \frac{60 \cdot f_s}{\frac{1}{M} \sum_{k=1}^{M} (n_{k+1} - n_k)}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — ECG Heart Rate

**Given:**
- Sampling frequency $f_s = 250$ Hz
- Detected R-peak sample indices: $n_1 = 500$, $n_2 = 800$, $n_3 = 1100$, $n_4 = 1400$

**Step 1 — Compute each RR interval in samples.**

For cycle 1: $\Delta n_1 = n_2 - n_1 = 800 - 500 = 300$ samples
For cycle 2: $\Delta n_2 = n_3 - n_2 = 1100 - 800 = 300$ samples
For cycle 3: $\Delta n_3 = n_4 - n_3 = 1400 - 1100 = 300$ samples

**Step 2 — Convert sample count to time using $T = \Delta n / f_s$.**

$$T_1 = \frac{300}{250} = 1.200 \text{ s}$$
$$T_2 = \frac{300}{250} = 1.200 \text{ s}$$
$$T_3 = \frac{300}{250} = 1.200 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute instantaneous HR for each cycle using $\text{HR} = 60 / T$.**

$$\text{HR}_1 = \frac{60}{1.200} = 50.00 \text{ BPM}$$
$$\text{HR}_2 = \frac{60}{1.200} = 50.00 \text{ BPM}$$
$$\text{HR}_3 = \frac{60}{1.200} = 50.00 \text{ BPM}$$

**Step 4 — Compute mean HR.**

$$\overline{\text{HR}} = \frac{50.00 + 50.00 + 50.00}{3} = 50.00 \text{ BPM}$$

**Step 5 — Cross-check using direct formula.**

$$\overline{\text{HR}} = \frac{60 \cdot f_s}{\overline{\Delta n}} = \frac{60 \times 250}{300} = \frac{15000}{300} = 50.00 \text{ BPM} \;\;\checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Interpretation:** A heart rate of 50 BPM is **bradycardic** in a healthy adult at rest, but is normal for trained athletes. In the lab, students should report both the **per-cycle HR values** and the **mean HR** with appropriate units.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — PPG Heart Rate

**Given:**
- Sampling frequency $f_s = 100$ Hz
- Detected PPG systolic peak sample indices: $p_1 = 100$, $p_2 = 220$, $p_3 = 340$, $p_4 = 460$, $p_5 = 580$

**Step 1 — Compute pulse-to-pulse intervals in samples.**

$$\Delta p_1 = 220 - 100 = 120 \text{ samples}$$
$$\Delta p_2 = 340 - 220 = 120 \text{ samples}$$
$$\Delta p_3 = 460 - 340 = 120 \text{ samples}$$
$$\Delta p_4 = 580 - 460 = 120 \text{ samples}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to time using $T = \Delta p / f_s$.**

$$T_1 = T_2 = T_3 = T_4 = \frac{120}{100} = 1.200 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute pulse rate for each cycle.**

$$\text{PR}_k = \frac{60}{1.200} = 50.00 \text{ BPM for all } k$$

**Step 4 — Mean PR.**

$$\overline{\text{PR}} = 50.00 \text{ BPM}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** In a healthy subject at rest, the **PPG pulse rate** and the **ECG heart rate** should be **virtually identical** (small differences can arise from the **Pulse Transit Time (PTT)** — typically 200 – 300 ms — which is the time taken by the pressure wave to travel from the heart to the peripheral measurement site).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Detection Pipeline Comparison

| Stage | ECG Processing | PPG Processing | Key Difference |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sampling Rate** | 250 – 1000 Hz | 100 – 500 Hz | ECG needs higher rate due to sharp QRS |
| **Dominant Feature** | R-peak (sharp, narrow) | Systolic peak (broad, smooth) | R-peak ≈ 100 ms wide; S-peak ≈ 300 ms wide |
| **Bandpass Filter** | 0.5 – 40 Hz | 0.5 – 8 Hz | ECG needs wider bandpass |
| **Peak Detection Difficulty** | Moderate (QRS amidst P, T) | Easy (smooth, isolated peaks) | PPG is generally easier |
| **Noise Susceptibility** | Powerline, EMG, baseline | Motion artifact dominates | PPG is more vulnerable to motion |
| **Algorithm of Choice** | Pan-Tompkins | `findpeaks` with smoothing | Different complexity |
| **Cardiac Event Tracked** | Electrical depolarization | Mechanical blood volume change | Different physical origins |
| **HRV Sensitivity** | Very high (gold standard) | Moderate (pre-ejection period noise) | ECG preferred for HRV studies |

## 3.5 MATLAB Code Walkthrough (Lab Implementation)

The following structured MATLAB snippet illustrates the canonical pipeline used in the KTU lab. (This is a **signal processing** lab — MATLAB is the KTU-prescribed tool; the program is included for pedagogical clarity, not for memorization of syntax.)

```matlab
%% EXPERIMENT 2: HEART RATE FROM ECG AND PPG
clc; clear; close all;

%% ---- 1. LOAD ECG SIGNAL ----
load('ecg_sample.mat');         % Provides 'ecg' and 'fs_ecg'
ecg = ecg(:);                   % Ensure column vector
t_ecg = (0:length(ecg)-1)/fs_ecg;

%% ---- 2. ECG PREPROCESSING ----
ecg_filt = bandpass(ecg, [0.5 40], fs_ecg);   % Bandpass filter

%% ---- 3. R-PEAK DETECTION ----
[~, r_locs] = findpeaks(ecg_filt, 'MinPeakHeight', 0.5*max(ecg_filt), ...
                              'MinPeakDistance', round(0.3*fs_ecg));

%% ---- 4. RR INTERVALS & HR ----
rr_samples   = diff(r_locs);                        % Samples
rr_seconds  = rr_samples / fs_ecg;                  % Seconds
hr_ecg      = 60 ./ rr_seconds;                     % BPM
mean_hr_ecg = mean(hr_ecg);
fprintf('Mean HR from ECG = %.2f BPM\n', mean_hr_ecg);

%% ---- 5. PPG PROCESSING (same structure) ----
load('ppg_sample.mat');         % Provides 'ppg' and 'fs_ppg'
ppg_filt = bandpass(ppg, [0.5 8], fs_ppg);
[~, s_locs] = findpeaks(ppg_filt, 'MinPeakDistance', round(0.3*fs_ppg));
pp_seconds = diff(s_locs) / fs_ppg;
mean_hr_ppg = mean(60 ./ pp_seconds);
fprintf('Mean HR from PPG = %.2f BPM\n', mean_hr_ppg);
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipeline Summary in Words:** *Load → Filter → Find Peaks → Measure Intervals → Compute 60/T → Average.* This five-step mantra applies to **every** heart rate detection algorithm in clinical practice and in wearable devices.

## 3.6 The Pan-Tompkins Derivative Operator Derivation

The five-point derivative used in Pan-Tompkins is an approximation of the first derivative $dy/dt$ of a discrete signal:

$$y[n] = \frac{1}{8}\big(2x[n+1] + x[n] - x[n-1] - 2x[n-2]\big)$$

This is derived from the Taylor series expansion of $x[n+1]$ and $x[n-2]$ up to the linear term:

$$x[n+1] \approx x[n] + T x'[n]$$
$$x[n-2] \approx x[n] - 2T x'[n]$$

where $T$ is the sampling period. Combining and solving for $x'[n]$:

$$x'[n] \approx \frac{1}{8T}\big(2x[n+1] + x[n] - x[n-1] - 2x[n-2]\big)$$

The $\frac{1}{8T}$ factor is absorbed into the gain of the next stage. The **purpose** of differentiation is to **suppress low-frequency P and T waves** (which have gentle slopes) while **amplifying the steep QRS slopes**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1 — Complete Heart Rate Detection Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid

    H --> I[COMPUTE INTERVALS RR OR PP]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2 Lab]**
**"Define Heart Rate. List the standard fiducial points used for HR computation from ECG and PPG signals."**

**Model Answer:**

**Heart Rate (HR)** is defined as the number of cardiac cycles completed by the heart per unit time, conventionally expressed in **Beats Per Minute (BPM)**. The mathematical expression is:

$$\text{HR (BPM)} = \frac{60}{T_{\text{cycle}}}$$

where $T_{\text{cycle}}$ is the period of one cardiac cycle in seconds.

**Fiducial Points:**
1. **For ECG**: The **R-peak** of the QRS complex is the primary fiducial point because it is the most prominent, sharpest, and most reliably detectable feature in the cardiac cycle. HR is computed as the reciprocal of the **R-R interval** (time between two successive R-peaks).
2. **For PPG**: The **systolic peak** (maximum amplitude of the pulse waveform) is the primary fiducial point. HR is computed as the reciprocal of the **peak-to-peak interval** between consecutive systolic peaks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the R-peak?** The R-peak corresponds to ventricular depolarization, which involves the largest muscle mass, produces the largest electrical potential, and has the steepest slope — all of which make it stand out from P, Q, S, and T waves, as well as from baseline noise.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2 Lab]**
**"Briefly explain the preprocessing steps required before R-peak detection in an ECG signal."**

**Model Answer:**

ECG signals are contaminated by multiple noise sources. Before R-peak detection, the following preprocessing steps are mandatory:

1. **Baseline Wander Removal**: Caused by respiration and electrode impedance changes (frequency: 0 – 0.5 Hz). Removed by a **high-pass filter** at 0.5 Hz or by **polynomial detrending**.

2. **Powerline Interference Removal**: 50/60 Hz sinusoidal noise from electrical mains. Removed by a **notch filter** centered at the line frequency.

3. **High-Frequency Noise Removal**: EMG artifacts and thermal noise above 40 Hz. Removed by a **low-pass filter** at 40 Hz.

4. **Combined Bandpass Filtering**: A single **bandpass filter of 0.5 – 40 Hz** simultaneously addresses points 1, 2, and 3, and is the standard preprocessing in clinical ECG analyzers.

After bandpass filtering, the QRS complex — whose dominant energy lies in the **5 – 15 Hz** band — is well isolated and ready for peak detection.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCBML507, Module 2]**
**"With a neat block diagram, explain the step-by-step procedure for detecting R-peaks and computing heart rate from an ECG signal. Apply your procedure to compute the mean heart rate from the following detected R-peak sample indices (assume $f_s = 250$ Hz): $n_1 = 250$, $n_2 = 550$, $n_3 = 850$, $n_4 = 1150$."**

#### Part (a) — Procedure and Block Diagram (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Signal Acquisition:**
Acquire the ECG signal using surface electrodes in a standard lead configuration (Lead II is most common for HR monitoring). Digitize the signal at a sampling frequency $f_s \geq 250$ Hz.

**Step 2 — Preprocessing:**
Apply a **bandpass filter (0.5 – 40 Hz)** to remove baseline wander, powerline interference, and high-frequency noise. This step is essential because raw ECG contains overlapping noise that can create false peaks.

**Step 3 — R-Peak Enhancement (Pan-Tompkins style):**
- **Differentiate** the filtered signal to emphasize the steep QRS slopes.
- **Square** the differentiated signal to make all values positive and amplify large excursions.
- **Integrate** over a moving window of approximately **150 ms** to produce a single smooth pulse per QRS complex.

**Step 4 — Peak Detection:**
Use MATLAB's `findpeaks` (or an adaptive thresholding scheme) to locate the R-peaks. Impose a **minimum peak distance** of 0.3 s (≈ 200 BPM upper limit) to prevent double-counting.

**Step 5 — RR Interval Calculation:**
For each pair of consecutive R-peaks, compute the R-R interval:
$$T_{\text{RR}}^{(k)} = \frac{n_{k+1} - n_k}{f_s}$$

**Step 6 — Heart Rate Computation:**
$$\text{HR}^{(k)} = \frac{60}{T_{\text{RR}}^{(k)}} \quad \text{(in BPM)}$$

**Step 7 — Averaging:**
Compute the mean HR over all detected cycles for a robust estimate:
$$\overline{\text{HR}} = \frac{60}{\overline{T_{\text{RR}}}}$$

**Block Diagram:**

```
   RAW ECG → BANDPASS FILTER (0.5–40 Hz) → DIFFERENTIATOR →
   SQUARER → MOVING AVERAGE (150 ms) → ADAPTIVE THRESHOLD →
   PEAK LOCATOR → RR INTERVAL → HR COMPUTATION (60/T) → DISPLAY
```

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given data:**
- $f_s = 250$ Hz
- R-peak indices: $n_1 = 250$, $n_2 = 550$, $n_3 = 850$, $n_4 = 1150$

**Step 1 — Compute differences in samples:**

$$\Delta n_1 = 550 - 250 = 300 \text{ samples}$$
$$\Delta n_2 = 850 - 550 = 300 \text{ samples}$$
$$\Delta n_3 = 1150 - 850 = 300 \text{ samples}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to seconds using $T = \Delta n / f_s$:**

$$T_1 = \frac{300}{250} = 1.200 \text{ s}$$
$$T_2 = \frac{300}{250} = 1.200 \text{ s}$$
$$T_3 = \frac{300}{250} = 1.200 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute per-cycle HR using $\text{HR} = 60 / T$:**

$$\text{HR}_1 = \frac{60}{1.200} = 50.00 \text{ BPM}$$
$$\text{HR}_2 = \frac{60}{1.200} = 50.00 \text{ BPM}$$
$$\text{HR}_3 = \frac{60}{1.200} = 50.00 \text{ BPM}$$

**Step 4 — Mean HR:**

$$\overline{\text{HR}} = \frac{50.00 + 50.00 + 50.00}{3} = \boxed{50.00 \text{ BPM}}$$

**Step 5 — Verification using direct formula:**

$$\overline{\text{HR}} = \frac{60 \cdot f_s}{\overline{\Delta n}} = \frac{60 \times 250}{300} = \frac{15000}{300} = 50.00 \text{ BPM} \;\;\checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question A):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to convert samples to seconds before dividing 60 — students often compute $\text{HR} = 60 / 300 = 0.2$ BPM, which is absurd. Always divide by $T$ in **seconds**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Not mentioning the **bandpass filter cutoffs** explicitly. The examiner expects **0.5 Hz and 40 Hz**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing **sampling frequency** with **Nyquist frequency** ($f_N = f_s/2 = 125$ Hz in this case). The 40 Hz cutoff is well below Nyquist.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to state **units (BPM)** in the final answer. KTU deducts 0.5 marks for missing units.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Not drawing a **labeled block diagram** for part (a). The block diagram carries at least 2–3 marks in the 7-mark sub-part.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCBML507, Module 2]**
**"Explain the Pan-Tompkins algorithm for QRS detection in detail. Compare the use of ECG and PPG signals for heart rate measurement, highlighting at least three differences."**

#### Part (a) — Pan-Tompkins Algorithm in Detail (7 Marks)

The **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** (J. Pan & W. Tompkins, 1985) is a real-time QRS detection algorithm consisting of **five cascaded stages**:

**Stage 1 — Bandpass Filtering (5 – 15 Hz):**
A cascaded low-pass filter (cutoff 11 Hz, order 2) and high-pass filter (cutoff 5 Hz, order 2) is applied. This isolates the QRS energy band while suppressing P and T waves, baseline wander, EMG noise, and powerline interference. The cascaded design provides a **passband gain of ≈ 36** and a sharp roll-off.

**Stage 2 — Derivative Operator:**
A five-point derivative is applied:
$$y[n] = \frac{1}{8}\big(2x[n+1] + x[n] - x[n-1] - 2x[n-2]\big)$$
This suppresses the low-frequency P and T waves (which have gentle slopes) and emphasizes the steep slopes of the QRS complex.

**Stage 3 — Squaring:**
$$y[n] = (x[n])^2$$
This makes all values non-negative, amplifies the QRS further (since squaring emphasizes large values), and provides nonlinear amplification.

**Stage 4 — Moving Window Integration:**
$$y[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[n-k]$$
where $N$ is the number of samples in a 150 ms window ($N = 0.15 \cdot f_s$). This produces a single smooth pulse per QRS, with its **rising edge marking the QRS onset** and **falling edge marking the QRS offset**.

**Stage 5 — Adaptive Thresholding:**
Two thresholds (signal level and noise level) are used. When an integration output exceeds the signal threshold, a QRS is detected. The thresholds are **continuously updated** based on the running estimates of signal and noise levels, providing robustness against amplitude and noise variations.

**Output:** The locations of detected R-peaks, from which RR intervals and heart rate are computed.

#### Part (b) — ECG vs. PPG for Heart Rate Measurement (7 Marks)

| Aspect | ECG | PPG |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1. Physical Principle** | Measures the **electrical depolarization** of the heart muscle via surface electrodes. | Measures **optical absorption/reflection** changes due to blood volume variations. |
| **2. Signal Morphology** | Sharp QRS complex; **R-peak** is narrow (~100 ms) and easy to identify. | Smooth pulse; **systolic peak** is broad (~300 ms) and may have a dicrotic notch. |
| **3. Sampling Rate Required** | Higher (250 – 1000 Hz) to resolve QRS. | Lower (100 – 500 Hz) is sufficient. |
| **4. Noise Sensitivity** | Powerline, EMG, baseline wander. | **Motion artifact** dominates; less sensitive to electrical noise. |
| **5. Contact Requirement** | Requires **multiple electrodes** with gel; needs skin preparation. | Requires a **single finger/wrist clip**; non-invasive and easier to apply. |
| **6. Suitability for Wearables** | Holter monitors (24-h recording). | Ideal for **smartwatches and fitness bands**. |
| **7. HRV Accuracy** | **Gold standard** for HRV (used in clinical studies). | Affected by pre-ejection period variability; less accurate. |
| **8. Diagnostic Information** | Reveals arrhythmias, ischemia, conduction disorders. | Limited mainly to pulse rate and oxygen saturation. |

**Conclusion:** ECG is the **clinical gold standard** for HR and HRV measurement, providing rich diagnostic information. PPG is the **consumer-electronics standard**, offering convenience, comfort, and continuous monitoring capability, with sufficient accuracy for fitness and wellness applications. The two modalities are **complementary**, not competing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing the **bandpass cutoffs** (5–15 Hz for Pan-Tompkins) with the **general ECG preprocessing cutoffs** (0.5–40 Hz). They are different stages with different purposes.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to explain **why each stage is needed** (not just *what* it does). KTU examiners award marks for the *rationale* behind each step.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In the comparison table, students often write **subjective opinions** ("ECG is better") instead of **objective technical differences** (sampling rate, principle, morphology, etc.). Always compare on **measurable engineering parameters**.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Skipping the **adaptive thresholding** stage description. This is a **frequently tested 1.5–2 mark component** in KTU papers.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Not writing a **concluding statement** in the comparison answer. A one-line conclusion summarizing when to use which modality is worth 0.5 marks.

---

## 5.6 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes — Must Memorize for KTU Exam:**

- **HR Definition**: $\text{HR (BPM)} = \dfrac{60}{T_{\text{cycle}} \text{ (seconds)}}$
- **Discrete HR Formula**: $\text{HR} = \dfrac{60 \cdot f_s}{N_{\text{samples between peaks}}}$
- **ECG Fiducial Point for HR**: **R-peak** of the QRS complex.
- **PPG Fiducial Point for HR**: **Systolic peak** (maximum amplitude).
- **ECG Bandpass for QRS Energy**: **5 – 15 Hz** (Pan-Tompkins stage 1).
- **ECG Bandpass for General Preprocessing**: **0.5 – 40 Hz**.
- **PPG Bandpass**: **0.5 – 8 Hz** (because HR range 40–200 BPM maps to 0.67–3.33 Hz).
- **Pan-Tompkins 5 Stages**: Bandpass → Derivative → Square → MA Integrate → Adaptive Threshold.
- **MA Window Length**: **150 ms** (i.e., $N = 0.15 \cdot f_s$ samples).
- **Minimum Peak Distance**: **0.3 s** (prevents double-detection; corresponds to 200 BPM upper limit).
- **Normal Resting HR**: **60 – 100 BPM**.
- **Bradycardia**: **HR < 60 BPM**.
- **Tachycardia**: **HR > 100 BPM**.
- **PPG ↔ ECG**: In healthy subjects, **PPG pulse rate ≈ ECG heart rate**, with a small offset equal to the **Pulse Transit Time (PTT)**.
- **Sampling Rates**: ECG ≥ **250 Hz**; PPG ≥ **100 Hz** (Nyquist-compliant).
- **Pipeline Mnemonic**: **L–F–F–D–A** → **L**oad, **F**ilter, **F**ind peaks, **D**iff (intervals), **A**verage.
- **MATLAB Functions to Remember**: `bandpass`, `findpeaks`, `diff`, `detrend`, `filtfilt`, `butter`.
- **Stable HR Computation**: Average the **intervals first**, then compute $\dfrac{60}{\overline{T}}$ (more robust than averaging BPMs).
- **Validation Trick**: Always verify with **SDNN** or visual inspection — a constant HR of exactly 50.00 BPM across all cycles in exam data is a **hint that the numbers were chosen to make arithmetic clean**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Mantra for This Experiment:**
> *"Filter, Find, Diff, Divide by Seconds, Multiply by Sixty, Average — and never forget the units."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processing of ECG signals for acquiring parameters like heart rate, QRS complex, P wave, etc.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML507 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Processing of ECG signals for acquiring parameters like heart rate, QRS complex, P wave, etc.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Electrocardiogram (ECG/EKG)** is a non-invasive bioelectrical recording of the heart's electrical activity over time, captured via surface electrodes placed on the limbs and chest. The signal represents the **summed action potentials** of millions of cardiac myocytes (specialized muscle cells) depolarizing and repolarizing in a coordinated sequence during each cardiac cycle.

In the context of **Biomedical Signal Processing (PCBML507)**, ECG signal processing refers to the application of digital filters, transform-domain techniques, and adaptive thresholding algorithms to extract clinically relevant temporal and morphological parameters such as the **P wave, QRS complex, T wave, PR interval, ST segment, QT interval, and instantaneous heart rate (HR)**.

The standard ECG paper speed is **25 mm/s** with vertical calibration of **10 mm/mV**, which directly maps physical time and voltage into printable graph paper coordinates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Focus:** Module Experiment 2 emphasizes algorithmic extraction of morphological features (P, QRS, T) and computation of heart rate from the **RR interval** (the time between successive R-peaks). The benchmark algorithm tested is the **Pan-Tompkins QRS Detection Algorithm (1985)**.

## 1.2 The ECG Waveform Components — A Complete Anatomy

A single normal cardiac cycle (PQRST complex) contains five major waves and four major intervals/segments:

| Component | Physiological Origin | Typical Duration | Typical Amplitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P wave** | Atrial depolarization (both atria) | 80–120 ms | 0.25 mV |
| **PR Segment** | AV nodal conduction delay | 50–120 ms | Isoelectric (0 mV) |
| **QRS Complex** | Ventricular depolarization | 80–120 ms | 1.0–1.5 mV (R-peak) |
| **ST Segment** | Ventricular plateau phase | 80–120 ms | Isoelectric (0 mV) |
| **T wave** | Ventricular repolarization | 160 ms | 0.1–0.5 mV |
| **U wave** | Purkinje repolarization (sometimes) | 160 ms | < 0.1 mV |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the QRS is the "anchor" of ECG processing:** Because ventricular muscle mass is much larger than atrial muscle mass, the QRS complex has the **highest amplitude** and **steepest slope (highest dV/dt)** in the ECG. This makes it the easiest feature to detect reliably, and almost all higher-level feature detection (P wave, T wave, HR) is performed *relative* to detected R-peaks.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Heart as an Orchestra"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition for first-time learners:** Think of the heart as a **symphony orchestra** with three sections — the atria (violins), the AV node (conductor's pause), and the ventricles (brass/drums). The ECG is the **conductor's score** that records the timing of every section.
> 
> - The **P wave** is the violins warming up (soft, gentle entry).
> - The **PR segment** is the dramatic pause where the conductor raises the baton.
> - The **QRS complex** is the loud, sharp brass+drum entry (LARGEST amplitude).
> - The **ST segment** is the sustained chord.
> - The **T wave** is the gentle decrescendo as the piece ends.
> 
> Signal processing is like the **sound engineer** who listens to a noisy recording of the orchestra and must reliably detect "when did the drums hit?" — once the drums (R-peaks) are found, everything else can be timed relative to them.

## 1.4 Heart Rate — The Primary Diagnostic Parameter

**Heart Rate (HR)** is the number of cardiac cycles per minute, computed from the **RR interval** (the time between two successive R-peaks):

$$HR = \frac{60}{RR_{interval}} \quad \text{[beats per minute, bpm]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clinical Reference Ranges (Must be memorized for KTU viva):**
> - **Normal Sinus Rhythm (NSR):** 60–100 bpm
> - **Bradycardia:** < 60 bpm
> - **Tachycardia:** > 100 bpm
> - **Athletic heart:** can be 40–60 bpm (normal variant)

The standard sampling frequency for digital ECG is **360 Hz** (per MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database) or **500 Hz** (per KTU laboratory convention), and the **QRS detection accuracy benchmark** is **> 99.5% sensitivity and positive predictivity** (Pan & Tompkins, 1985).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Pan-Tompkins Algorithm — Operational Philosophy

The **Pan-Tompkins Algorithm (1985)** is the *de facto* industry-standard for real-time QRS detection. It is a **multi-stage non-linear digital filter** designed to maximize the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of the QRS complex relative to noise sources like:

- **Powerline interference** (50/60 Hz)
- **Baseline wander** (0.15–0.5 Hz, due to respiration and electrode motion)
- **Muscle noise (EMG)** (20–2000 Hz)
- **High-frequency instrumentation noise**

The algorithm achieves detection by exploiting **three physical properties** of the QRS complex that distinguish it from noise and other ECG waves:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Cardinal Properties of QRS Used in Pan-Tompkins:**
> 1. **Frequency concentration** — QRS energy is concentrated in **5–15 Hz**.
> 2. **High slope (dV/dt)** — The QRS has the steepest rising and falling edges of any wave.
> 3. **Higher amplitude after non-linear transformation** — Squaring emphasizes the dominant QRS over P and T waves.

## 2.2 Stage-by-Stage Operational Breakdown

The algorithm is a **five-stage cascade**:

### **Stage 1 — Bandpass Filtering (5–15 Hz)**
A cascade of a **second-order low-pass filter** and a **second-order high-pass filter** isolates the QRS energy band.

**Low-pass filter (cutoff ≈ 11 Hz, gain = 36):**

$$y_L(n) = 2y_L(n-1) - y_L(n-2) + x(n) - 2x(n-6) + x(n-12)$$

**High-pass filter (cutoff ≈ 5 Hz):**

$$y_H(n) = y_H(n-1) + \frac{x(n-16) - x(n-17) - x(n) + x(n-32)}{32}$$

The combined passband **suppresses baseline drift** (removes frequencies below 5 Hz) and **filters EMG/powerline noise** (removes frequencies above 15 Hz).

### **Stage 2 — Derivative Filter (Slope Amplifier)**
A five-point derivative operator approximates $dV/dt$, the slope of the signal, which is maximal at the QRS edges:

$$y_D(n) = \frac{1}{8}\left[-x(n-2) - 2x(n-1) + 2x(n+1) + x(n+2)\right]$$

This step **suppresses P and T waves** (which have gentle slopes) and **amplifies the QRS** (which has steep slopes).

### **Stage 3 — Squaring Operation (Non-linear Amplifier)**
A point-wise squaring of the derivative output:

$$y_S(n) = \left[y_D(n)\right]^2$$

This **makes all values positive** and **non-linearly amplifies larger slopes**, drastically enhancing the QRS relative to P and T waves.

### **Stage 4 — Moving Window Integration (Smoothed Envelope)**
A moving average over a window of width $N$ (typically $N = 0.15 \times f_s$ samples, where $f_s$ is the sampling rate):

$$y_I(n) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} y_S(n-k)$$

This produces a **smoothed pulse** whose peak corresponds to the R-peak.

### **Stage 5 — Adaptive Thresholding**
The integrated output is compared against two adaptive thresholds (high and low). A QRS is detected when the signal crosses the high threshold; the low threshold is used to capture missed beats in noisy segments.

$$THR_{high}(n) = TH_1 \cdot y_I(peak) + TH_2 \cdot Noise\_Estimate$$
$$THR_{low}(n) = TL_1 \cdot y_I(peak) + TL_2 \cdot Noise\_Estimate$$

The noise estimate and signal level are updated recursively based on the running mean of the integrated output and the detected RR intervals.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heart Rate | $HR = \dfrac{60}{RR_{interval}}$ | bpm | $RR_{interval}$ in seconds |
| 2 | Heart Rate (samples) | $HR = \dfrac{60 \cdot f_s}{N_{RR}}$ | bpm | $N_{RR}$ = samples between R-peaks, $f_s$ = Hz |
| 3 | Sampling Freq (MIT-BIH) | $f_s = 360$ | Hz | KTU default |
| 4 | Sampling Freq (KSKV lab) | $f_s = 500$ | Hz | KTU lab convention |
| 5 | QRS Bandwidth | $BW_{QRS} = 5 \text{ to } 15$ | Hz | Pan-Tompkins passband |
| 6 | Baseline Wander | $f < 0.5$ | Hz | Removed by high-pass |
| 7 | Powerline Interference | $f = 50$ | Hz | India standard (60 Hz in US) |
| 8 | Integration Window | $N = 0.15 \cdot f_s$ | samples | ≈ 150 ms window |
| 9 | RR Interval (Normal) | $0.6 \le RR \le 1.0$ | s | Corresponds to 60–100 bpm |
| 10 | QRS Duration (Normal) | $0.08 \le QRS \le 0.12$ | s | Wide QRS > 0.12s = pathology |
| 11 | PR Interval (Normal) | $0.12 \le PR \le 0.20$ | s | AV conduction time |
| 12 | QT Interval (Normal) | $0.35 \le QT \le 0.44$ | s | Corrected via Bazett: $QTc = QT / \sqrt{RR}$ |
| 13 | Derivative Output | $y_D(n) = \dfrac{1}{8}\left[-x(n-2) - 2x(n-1) + 2x(n+1) + x(n+2)\right]$ | V/sample | Five-point operator |
| 14 | Squaring | $y_S(n) = \left[y_D(n)\right]^2$ | V² | Non-linear amplifier |
| 15 | Moving Average | $y_I(n) = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} y_S(n-k)$ | V² | Smoothed envelope |
| 16 | Time-Frequency | $T \cdot \Delta f \ge \dfrac{1}{2}$ | dimensionless | Uncertainty principle |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** Pan-Tompkins is the workhorse algorithm inside virtually every **Holter monitor, ICD (Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator), pacemaker Holter telemetry, and wearable fitness ECG** (e.g., Apple Watch, AliveCor KardiaMobile). Its low computational cost makes it suitable for **real-time embedded DSP** on microcontrollers.

## 2.4 P-Wave and T-Wave Detection Strategies

P and T waves are **5–10× smaller** than the QRS and have **gentler slopes**, so they are not directly detected by Pan-Tompkins. Instead, they are located using **search windows** defined relative to the R-peak:

- **P-wave search window:** $[R_n - 0.25\text{ s}, R_n - 0.05\text{ s}]$ — i.e., look ~250 ms before the R-peak, but not too close (avoid Q-wave contamination).
- **T-wave search window:** $[R_n + 0.10\text{ s}, R_n + 0.40\text{ s}]$ — i.e., look 100–400 ms after the R-peak.

The maximum absolute amplitude within each window is marked as the **P-peak** and **T-peak**, respectively.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Heart Rate from RR Interval

### Step 1 — Definition of RR Interval
The **RR interval** is the elapsed time between the apexes of two consecutive R-waves:

$$RR_n = t_{R,n+1} - t_{R,n} \quad \text{[seconds]}$$

### Step 2 — Relationship to Number of Beats
If one cardiac cycle takes $RR_n$ seconds, then in 60 seconds, the number of cycles is:

$$N_{cycles} = \frac{60}{RR_n} \quad \text{[beats per minute]}$$

### Step 3 — Recognition that this IS the heart rate
By definition, the heart rate is the number of beats per minute, hence:

$$HR_n = \frac{60}{RR_n} \quad \text{[bpm]}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to discrete samples (for sampled ECG)
If the sampling frequency is $f_s$ Hz, then each sample represents $\dfrac{1}{f_s}$ seconds. If the R-peaks occur at sample indices $k_{R,n}$ and $k_{R,n+1}$, the number of samples between them is:

$$N_{RR} = k_{R,n+1} - k_{R,n}$$

Therefore the time between R-peaks is:

$$RR_n = \frac{N_{RR}}{f_s} \quad \text{[seconds]}$$

Substituting into the HR equation:

$$HR_n = \frac{60}{N_{RR}/f_s} = \frac{60 \cdot f_s}{N_{RR}} \quad \text{[bpm]}$$

### Numerical Example (KTU-style)
Given: $f_s = 360$ Hz, $N_{RR} = 432$ samples.

$$RR = \frac{432}{360} = 1.2 \text{ s}$$

$$HR = \frac{60}{1.2} = 50 \text{ bpm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value of 50 bpm falls in the **bradycardia** range — a clinically meaningful flag for the student to report.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Five-Point Derivative Operator

The goal is to approximate $\dfrac{dV}{dt}$ at sample $n$ using a centered finite difference.

### Step 1 — Taylor Series Expansion (forward at $n+1$)
$$x(n+1) = x(n) + T \cdot x'(n) + \frac{T^2}{2} x''(n) + \frac{T^3}{6} x'''(n) + O(T^4)$$

### Step 2 — Taylor Series Expansion (forward at $n+2$)
$$x(n+2) = x(n) + 2T \cdot x'(n) + \frac{(2T)^2}{2} x''(n) + \frac{(2T)^3}{6} x'''(n) + O(T^4)$$

### Step 3 — Taylor Series Expansion (backward at $n-1$ and $n-2$)
$$x(n-1) = x(n) - T \cdot x'(n) + \frac{T^2}{2} x''(n) - \frac{T^3}{6} x'''(n) + O(T^4)$$

$$x(n-2) = x(n) - 2T \cdot x'(n) + \frac{(2T)^2}{2} x''(n) - \frac{(2T)^3}{6} x'''(n) + O(T^4)$$

### Step 4 — Form a Linear Combination
Take: $-x(n-2) - 2x(n-1) + 2x(n+1) + x(n+2)$

Expanding using the above:

$$-x(n-2) = -x(n) + 2T x'(n) - 2T^2 x''(n) + \frac{4T^3}{3} x'''(n)$$

$$-2x(n-1) = -2x(n) + 2T x'(n) - T^2 x''(n) + \frac{T^3}{3} x'''(n)$$

$$2x(n+1) = 2x(n) + 2T x'(n) + T^2 x''(n) + \frac{T^3}{3} x'''(n)$$

$$x(n+2) = x(n) + 2T x'(n) + 2T^2 x''(n) + \frac{4T^3}{3} x'''(n)$$

### Step 5 — Sum the four expansions

**Constant terms ($x(n)$):** $-1 - 2 + 2 + 1 = 0$ ✓ (cancels DC)

**$x'(n)$ terms:** $2T + 2T + 2T + 2T = 8T$

**$x''(n)$ terms:** $-2T^2 - T^2 + T^2 + 2T^2 = 0$ ✓ (cancels even-order errors)

**$x'''(n)$ terms:** $\frac{4T^3}{3} + \frac{T^3}{3} + \frac{T^3}{3} + \frac{4T^3}{3} = \frac{10T^3}{3}$

So:

$$-x(n-2) - 2x(n-1) + 2x(n+1) + x(n+2) = 8T \cdot x'(n) + \frac{10T^3}{3} x'''(n) + O(T^5)$$

### Step 6 — Solve for the derivative
Dividing both sides by $8T$:

$$x'(n) \approx \frac{-x(n-2) - 2x(n-1) + 2x(n+1) + x(n+2)}{8T}$$

In Pan-Tompkins, the constant $1/(8T)$ is folded into the gain of the derivative filter, yielding:

$$\boxed{y_D(n) = \frac{1}{8}\left[-x(n-2) - 2x(n-1) + 2x(n+1) + x(n+2)\right]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a five-point operator?** The fourth-order Taylor expansion terms cancel **automatically** (the $x''(n)$ coefficients sum to zero), giving a much cleaner derivative estimate than the simple two-point difference $\frac{x(n+1) - x(n-1)}{2T}$. The even-order error cancellation is the key mathematical strength of this operator.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Moving Window Integration

The moving average over $N$ samples is the **convolution** of $y_S(n)$ with a rectangular window of length $N$:

$$y_I(n) = (y_S * h)(n), \quad h(k) = \frac{1}{N} \text{ for } 0 \le k \le N-1$$

Expanding the convolution:

$$y_I(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k) \cdot y_S(n-k) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} y_S(n-k)$$

The choice $N = 0.15 \cdot f_s$ ensures the integration window is approximately **150 ms wide**, which is **wider than a typical QRS** (≈ 100 ms) but **narrower than the distance between R and T waves** (≈ 200–300 ms). This guarantees that the integrated waveform has a clear single peak at the R-peak location without merging the T-wave.

**Derivation of the choice $N = 0.15 \cdot f_s$:**
- QRS duration ≈ 100–120 ms (we want the integration window ≥ QRS width).
- T-wave begins ≈ 200 ms after R-peak (we want the integration window ≤ this gap).
- The compromise $N \approx 150$ ms = $0.15$ s is the **median** value, scaled by $f_s$ to convert time to samples.

$$N = 0.15 \cdot f_s$$

For $f_s = 360$ Hz: $N = 54$ samples. For $f_s = 500$ Hz: $N = 75$ samples.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Detection Sensitivity Trade-offs

| Noise Source | Frequency | Pan-Tompkins Stage that Removes it | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Baseline wander (respiration) | 0.15–0.5 Hz | Stage 1 High-pass | Cutoff at 5 Hz |
| Powerline interference (India) | 50 Hz | Stage 1 Low-pass | Cutoff at 15 Hz |
| High-frequency EMG noise | 20–2000 Hz | Stage 1 Low-pass | Cutoff at 15 Hz |
| T-wave (false QRS trigger) | 0.5–8 Hz | Stage 2 Derivative | T-wave slope much lower |
| T-wave (false QRS trigger) | — | Stage 3 Squaring | Squared T is much smaller |
| Motion artifact | Wideband | Stage 5 Adaptive threshold | Raises THR to avoid false trigger |
| Tall P-wave (false QRS) | 0.5–8 Hz | Stage 4 Integration | Single peak (P-wave is too narrow/short) |
| **Refractory logic (RR < 200 ms)** | Logic | Stage 5 Post-processing | Reject detections within 200 ms of last R |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pan-Tompkins Block Diagram (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw ECG Signal xn] --> B[Stage 1 Bandpass Filter 5 to 15 Hz]
    B --> C[Stage 2 Five Point Derivative]
    C --> D[Stage 3 Squaring Operation]
    D --> E[Stage 4 Moving Window Integration]
    E --> F[Stage 5 Adaptive Thresholding]
    F --> G{QRS Detected?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Update Signal Level]
    G -- No --> I[Update Noise Level]
    H --> J[Record R peak Index]
    I --> F
    J --> K[Compute RR interval]
    K --> L[Compute Heart Rate HR equals 60 by RR]
    K --> M[Define P wave Search Window 250 ms before R]
    K --> N[Define T wave Search Window 100 to 400 ms after R]
    M --> O[Locate P peak in Window]
    N --> P[Locate T peak in Window]
    O --> Q[Output ECG Parameters]
    P --> Q
    L --> Q
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each rectangular box is a *processing stage*; the diamond `QRS Detected?` is a *decision point*; the loop back to Stage 5 from the "No" branch is the **feedback path** that updates the noise estimate for the adaptive threshold. This is what makes Pan-Tompkins *adaptive* rather than a fixed-threshold detector.

## 4.2 ECG PQRST Waveform with Annotated Features

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Normal_PQRST_Complex
        Pwave[P wave Atrial Depolarization 80 to 120 ms 0.25 mV]
        PRseg[PR Segment AV Nodal Delay 50 to 120 ms Isoelectric]
        QRS[QRS Complex Ventricular Depolarization 80 to 120 ms 1.0 mV R peak]
        STseg[ST Segment Ventricular Plateau 80 to 120 ms Isoelectric]
        Twave[T wave Ventricular Repolarization 160 ms 0.1 to 0.5 mV]
    end
    Pwave --> PRseg --> QRS --> STseg --> Twave
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the QRS the tallest wave?** Ventricular muscle mass is **~50–70 g** of synchronized tissue, whereas atrial mass is **~15–20 g**. Since the ECG voltage is proportional to the volume of depolarizing tissue, the QRS is **3–5× taller** than the P wave.

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of Parameter Extraction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Acquire ECG via ADS1292 or Biokit] --> B[Sample at fs equals 360 or 500 Hz]
    B --> C[Preprocessing Bandpass 0.5 to 40 Hz Notch 50 Hz]
    C --> D[Pan Tompkins Pipeline 5 Stages]
    D --> E[R peak Index Array]
    E --> F[HR Module compute 60 by RR]
    E --> G[QRS Module width and amplitude]
    E --> H[P Module search window before R]
    E --> I[T Module search window after R]
    E --> J[Interval Module PR QRS QT]
    F --> K[Display on LCD or PC GUI]
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Hardware mapping (KTU lab context):** The "ADS1292" block corresponds to the **Texas Instruments ADS1292R** front-end IC commonly used in KTU biomedical lab kits; "Biokit" refers to the **KSKV KTU-standard ECG biokit**. The signal is acquired at the front end, processed by a **DSP processor** (typically **TMS320C5505** or **ARM Cortex-M4**), and the extracted HR/QRS/P/T parameters are displayed in real time.
