# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define an ECG signal. List the major waves of a normal ECG cycle and state the physiological origin of each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Electrocardiogram (ECG):** A graphical recording of the electrical activity of the heart over time, obtained using surface electrodes placed on the body. It represents the sum of action potentials generated by the cardiac muscle during depolarization and repolarization.

**Major Waves and their Physiological Origin:**

| Wave | Origin | Duration |
|---|---|---|
| **P wave** | Atrial depolarization (contraction of atria) | 80–120 ms |
| **QRS complex** | Ventricular depolarization (contraction of ventricles) | 80–120 ms |
| **T wave** | Ventricular repolarization (relaxation of ventricles) | ~160 ms |

> [!NOTE]
> A small **U wave** (Purkinje fiber repolarization) may also be present; atrial repolarization is generally hidden inside the QRS complex.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is meant by the RR interval? How is heart rate computed from it? What is the normal heart rate range?**

**Model Answer:**

The **RR interval** is the time elapsed between two successive R-peaks in the ECG waveform, representing one complete cardiac cycle.

**Formula:**

$$HR = \frac{60}{RR_{interval}} \quad \text{[beats per minute]}$$

For sampled signals:

$$HR = \frac{60 \cdot f_s}{N_{RR}}$$

where $f_s$ is the sampling frequency and $N_{RR}$ is the number of samples between two consecutive R-peaks.

**Normal Heart Rate Range:**
- **Normal Sinus Rhythm:** 60–100 bpm
- **Bradycardia:** < 60 bpm
- **Tachycardia:** > 100 bpm

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. You must attempt **ONE** of the two alternatives. Each alternative is split into **(a) 7 marks** (theory/concept) and **(b) 7 marks** (numerical/derivation/diagram).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**With a neat block diagram, explain the Pan-Tompkins algorithm for QRS detection in ECG signals. State the role of each stage.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Pan-Tompkins algorithm (1985)** is a real-time QRS detection algorithm that uses a cascade of five processing stages to maximize the signal-to-noise ratio of the QRS complex.

**Block Diagram:**

```
ECG → [Bandpass 5-15 Hz] → [Derivative] → [Squaring] → [Moving Avg] → [Adaptive Threshold] → QRS Detected
```

**Role of Each Stage:**

1. **Bandpass Filter (5–15 Hz):** Isolates the frequency band where QRS energy is concentrated. Removes baseline wander (low-frequency noise from respiration) and high-frequency noise (EMG, powerline interference).

2. **Derivative Filter:** Approximates the slope ($dV/dt$) of the signal. The QRS complex has the steepest slope, so this stage amplifies the QRS while suppressing P and T waves (which have gentle slopes).

3. **Squaring Operation:** Point-wise squaring of the derivative output. Makes all values positive and non-linearly amplifies the dominant QRS, suppressing smaller waves.

4. **Moving Window Integration:** Smooths the squared signal by averaging over a window of width $N = 0.15 \cdot f_s$ samples. Produces a pulse whose peak corresponds to the R-peak.

5. **Adaptive Thresholding:** Two adaptive thresholds (high and low) are updated based on running estimates of the signal and noise levels. A QRS is detected when the integrated signal crosses the high threshold; the low threshold is used to recover missed beats in noisy segments.

**Post-processing:** A **refractory period** of 200 ms is enforced — detections within 200 ms of a previous R-peak are rejected, preventing false double-counts.

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**A digital ECG signal is sampled at $f_s = 500$ Hz. The R-peaks are detected at sample indices 1200, 1450, 1700, and 1950. Compute the instantaneous heart rate for each cardiac cycle. Comment on the rhythm.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute the number of samples in each RR interval:**

$$N_{RR,1} = 1450 - 1200 = 250 \text{ samples}$$
$$N_{RR,2} = 1700 - 1450 = 250 \text{ samples}$$
$$N_{RR,3} = 1950 - 1700 = 250 \text{ samples}$$

**Step 2 — Convert samples to time:**

$$RR_1 = \frac{250}{500} = 0.5 \text{ s}$$
$$RR_2 = \frac{250}{500} = 0.5 \text{ s}$$
$$RR_3 = \frac{250}{500} = 0.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Heart Rate for each cycle:**

$$HR_1 = \frac{60}{0.5} = 120 \text{ bpm}$$
$$HR_2 = \frac{60}{0.5} = 120 \text{ bpm}$$
$$HR_3 = \frac{60}{0.5} = 120 \text{ bpm}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on the rhythm:**

Since all RR intervals are **identical** (250 samples = 0.5 s) and the heart rate is **120 bpm** (> 100 bpm), this represents a **regular TACHYCARDIA**. The patient is in a state of elevated heart rate with no beat-to-beat variability, which may indicate sinus tachycardia, atrial tachycardia, or other supraventricular rhythms requiring clinical correlation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the physiological significance of P wave, QRS complex, and T wave in an ECG. Draw a labeled PQRST waveform and indicate the PR interval, QRS duration, and QT interval.**

**Model Answer:**

**P wave (Atrial Depolarization):**
- Generated when the SA node fires and the depolarization wave spreads across both atria.
- **Duration:** 80–120 ms
- **Amplitude:** ≤ 0.25 mV
- **Clinical significance:** Inversion in lead II may indicate retrograde atrial activation; tall peaked P waves may indicate atrial enlargement.

**QRS Complex (Ventricular Depolarization):**
- Generated when the depolarization wave passes through the AV node, bundle of His, bundle branches, and Purkinje fibers to depolarize the ventricular myocardium.
- **Duration:** 80–120 ms (wide QRS > 120 ms indicates bundle branch block or ventricular origin)
- **Amplitude:** 1.0–1.5 mV (R-peak in lead II)
- **Clinical significance:** Tall R-waves indicate ventricular hypertrophy; deep Q-waves indicate myocardial infarction.

**T wave (Ventricular Repolarization):**
- Generated as the ventricular myocardium returns to its resting polarized state.
- **Duration:** ~160 ms
- **Amplitude:** 0.1–0.5 mV
- **Clinical significance:** Inverted T-waves indicate ischemia; tall peaked T-waves indicate hyperkalemia.

**Labeled Waveform (text representation):**

```
        P           PR         R
        ___        Segment      /\
       /   \      _________   /  \
      /     \                /    \
  ___/       \______________/      \________
                                            \         T
                                             \       /\
                                              \     /  \____
                                               \___/        \____
                          |---PR Interval---|
                          |----QRS Duration---|
                          |----------QT Interval---------|
```

**Key Intervals:**

| Interval | Definition | Normal Duration |
|---|---|---|
| **PR Interval** | Start of P wave to start of QRS | 120–200 ms |
| **QRS Duration** | Start of Q wave to end of S wave | 80–120 ms |
| **QT Interval** | Start of QRS to end of T wave | 350–440 ms (rate-corrected) |

---

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Derive the five-point derivative operator used in the Pan-Tompkins algorithm. Show explicitly why the even-order error terms cancel.**

**Model Answer:**

We seek a linear combination of samples $x(n-2), x(n-1), x(n+1), x(n+2)$ that approximates $x'(n)$ to fourth order in $T$.

**Step 1 — Write Taylor expansions about $n$:**

$$x(n \pm 1) = x(n) \pm T x'(n) + \frac{T^2}{2} x''(n) \pm \frac{T^3}{6} x'''(n) + \frac{T^4}{24} x^{(4)}(n) + \ldots$$

$$x(n \pm 2) = x(n) \pm 2T x'(n) + \frac{(2T)^2}{2} x''(n) \pm \frac{(2T)^3}{6} x'''(n) + \frac{(2T)^4}{24} x^{(4)}(n) + \ldots$$

**Step 2 — Form the linear combination $L = -x(n-2) - 2x(n-1) + 2x(n+1) + x(n+2)$:**

Expanding term by term:

$$-x(n-2) = -x(n) + 2T x'(n) - 2T^2 x''(n) + \frac{4T^3}{3} x'''(n) - \frac{2T^4}{3} x^{(4)}(n)$$

$$-2x(n-1) = -2x(n) + 2T x'(n) - T^2 x''(n) + \frac{T^3}{3} x'''(n) - \frac{T^4}{12} x^{(4)}(n)$$

$$+2x(n+1) = +2x(n) + 2T x'(n) + T^2 x''(n) + \frac{T^3}{3} x'''(n) + \frac{T^4}{12} x^{(4)}(n)$$

$$+x(n+2) = +x(n) + 2T x'(n) + 2T^2 x''(n) + \frac{4T^3}{3} x'''(n) + \frac{2T^4}{3} x^{(4)}(n)$$

**Step 3 — Sum the four expansions:**

| Term | Coefficient |
|---|---|
| $x(n)$ | $-1 - 2 + 2 + 1 = 0$ |
| $T x'(n)$ | $2 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 8$ |
| $T^2 x''(n)$ | $-2 - 1 + 1 + 2 = 0$ ✓ **Even-order cancels** |
| $T^3 x'''(n)$ | $\frac{4}{3} + \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{3} + \frac{4}{3} = \frac{10}{3}$ |
| $T^4 x^{(4)}(n)$ | $-\frac{2}{3} - \frac{1}{12} + \frac{1}{12} + \frac{2}{3} = 0$ ✓ **Even-order cancels** |

**Step 4 — Solve for the derivative:**

$$L = 8T \cdot x'(n) + \frac{10T^3}{3} x'''(n) + O(T^5)$$

$$x'(n) = \frac{L - \frac{10T^3}{3} x'''(n) - \ldots}{8T} = \frac{L}{8T} - \frac{10T^2}{24} x'''(n) - \ldots$$

To leading order:

$$\boxed{x'(n) \approx \frac{1}{8T}\left[-x(n-2) - 2x(n-1) + 2x(n+1) + x(n+2)\right]}$$

The error is of order $T^2$ (the $x'''$ term), which is the same accuracy as the standard centered difference but with **automatic suppression of DC bias and very-low-frequency drift** because the $x(n)$ and $x''(n)$ coefficients both sum to zero. This makes the operator robust to baseline wander — a critical property for ECG processing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to cancel the constant term** $x(n)$ — if the sum is not zero, your operator has a DC bias and the derivative estimate drifts. The examiner will deduct 1–2 marks for not showing this cancellation.
> 2. **Incorrectly stating the operator is a "two-point difference"** — Pan-Tompkins specifically uses the FIVE-point operator for its superior accuracy. Writing $x(n+1) - x(n-1)$ instead will cost 2 marks.
> 3. **Missing the squaring stage** in the block diagram of Q3(a) — squaring is what makes Pan-Tompkins non-linear and is the key innovation that suppresses T-waves. Omitting it loses 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU Viva & Exam Revision — Bullet-Point Summary:**

### **ECG Anatomy (must-memorize)**
- ✅ **P wave** = Atrial depolarization, 80–120 ms, ≤ 0.25 mV
- ✅ **QRS complex** = Ventricular depolarization, 80–120 ms, ~1.0 mV (R-peak)
- ✅ **T wave** = Ventricular repolarization, ~160 ms, 0.1–0.5 mV
- ✅ **PR interval** = 120–200 ms (atria to ventricles)
- ✅ **QT interval** = 350–440 ms (corrected)
- ✅ **Atrial repolarization is hidden inside the QRS** (no separate wave)

### **Heart Rate (must-memorize formula)**
- ✅ $HR = \dfrac{60}{RR_{interval}}$ bpm
- ✅ $HR = \dfrac{60 \cdot f_s}{N_{RR}}$ for sampled ECG
- ✅ Normal: 60–100 bpm; Bradycardia: < 60; Tachycardia: > 100

### **Pan-Tompkins Algorithm (5 stages in order)**
- ✅ **Stage 1:** Bandpass 5–15 Hz (cascade of LPF + HPF)
- ✅ **Stage 2:** Five-point derivative (slope amplifier)
- ✅ **Stage 3:** Squaring (non-linear amplifier)
- ✅ **Stage 4:** Moving window integration (window = $0.15 \cdot f_s$)
- ✅ **Stage 5:** Adaptive thresholding (two thresholds: high + low)
- ✅ **Post-processing:** 200 ms refractory period

### **The 3 Cardinal Properties of QRS**
- ✅ Frequency concentrated in 5–15 Hz
- ✅ Steepest slope ($dV/dt$)
- ✅ Highest amplitude after squaring

### **P and T Wave Search Windows**
- ✅ P wave: 250 ms to 50 ms **before** the R-peak
- ✅ T wave: 100 ms to 400 ms **after** the R-peak

### **Sampling & Standards**
- ✅ MIT-BIH standard: $f_s = 360$ Hz
- ✅ KTU lab standard: $f_s = 500$ Hz
- ✅ Notch filter: 50 Hz (India powerline)
- ✅ ECG paper: 25 mm/s, 10 mm/mV
- ✅ **QRS detection benchmark:** > 99.5% sensitivity and positive predictivity

### **Common Viva Questions**
- ✅ "Why bandpass 5–15 Hz?" → Isolates QRS, removes baseline drift + EMG + 50 Hz interference
- ✅ "Why squaring?" → Makes all values positive + non-linearly amplifies large QRS slopes
- ✅ "Why 150 ms integration window?" → Wider than QRS (~100 ms) but narrower than R-T gap (~300 ms)
- ✅ "Why refractory period 200 ms?" → Prevents double-counting within one cardiac cycle
- ✅ "Why five-point derivative?" → Even-order error cancellation → robust to baseline drift
- ✅ "Why is the QRS tallest?" → Ventricular mass is 3–5× atrial mass
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Medical data compression (Lossy vs. Lossless signals)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML507 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Medical data compression (Lossy vs. Lossless signals)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Medical Data Compression** is the process of encoding biomedical signals (ECG, EEG, EMG) and medical images (MRI, CT, Ultrasound, X-ray) using fewer bits than the original representation, while preserving clinically relevant diagnostic information for storage, retrieval, and transmission over bandwidth-limited channels.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **PCBML507 – Biomedical Signal Processing Lab**, this experiment (Experiment 2) focuses on the comparative analysis of **Lossless** and **Lossy** compression schemes applied to physiological signals, evaluating the trade-off between **Compression Ratio (CR)** and **diagnostic fidelity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction (Board-Exam Favourite):**
> - **Lossless Compression:** Original signal/image can be **reconstructed EXACTLY** (bit-perfect recovery). Used when every sample carries clinical significance (e.g., ECG arrhythmia detection, legal medical records).
> - **Lossy Compression:** Original data **cannot be exactly recovered**; controlled information loss is accepted for higher compression. Used when minor loss is clinically tolerable (e.g., telemedicine, archived scans for trend analysis).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are packing a suitcase for a long trip:

- **Lossless compression** is like **folding clothes perfectly** (rolling, vacuum-seal bags). You can unpack them later and find every shirt in pristine condition — nothing is missing, nothing is destroyed. The suitcase is smaller, but the contents are 100% recoverable.
- **Lossy compression** is like **throwing away items you "probably won't need"** (an extra pair of socks, a low-priority book). Your suitcase is dramatically smaller, but when you arrive, those items are gone forever. The trade-off: **huge space savings vs. permanent information loss**.

In the biomedical world, an ECG trace of a heart patient is like a delicate, irreplaceable heirloom — you want **lossless**. A routine chest X-ray for a follow-up checkup is more like a magazine — **lossy** is fine if the diagnostic features are preserved.

### 1.3 Why Compression Matters in Biomedical Engineering

| Challenge | Raw Biomedical Data | Compression Benefit |
|---|---|---|
| 24-hour Holter ECG | ~1 GB continuous data | Reduced to 50–200 MB |
| MRI volume (512×512×200) | ~100 MB | Reduced to 5–20 MB |
| Hospital PACS storage | Petabytes/year | ~70–90% storage savings |
| Telemedicine bandwidth | Limited rural links | Faster transmission |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics You MUST Memorise for KTU Viva:**
> - **CR (Compression Ratio)** = Original Size / Compressed Size
> - **PRD (Percentage Root-mean-square Difference)** measures distortion in lossy compression
> - **SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio)** in dB indicates reconstruction quality

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Medical Data Compression Techniques

#### A. Lossless Compression (Reversible)
1. **Run-Length Encoding (RLE)** — effective for signals with long constant segments.
2. **Huffman Coding** — assigns shorter codes to frequent symbols (entropy-based).
3. **LZW (Lempel–Ziv–Welch)** — dictionary-based, used in GIF/TIFF medical formats.
4. **Arithmetic Coding** — superior to Huffman, near-optimal entropy coding.
5. **DPCM (Differential Pulse Code Modulation)** with entropy coding.
6. **PNG, DICOM (lossless mode)** — used in PACS for archival.

#### B. Lossy Compression (Irreversible)
1. **DCT-based compression** (JPEG for medical images).
2. **DWT (Discrete Wavelet Transform)** with thresholding — SPIHT, EZW algorithms.
3. **Vector Quantization (VQ)** — codebook-based.
4. **Sub-band Coding** — frequency-band decomposition.
5. **Compressed Sensing (CS)** — modern sparse-recovery approach.

### 2.2 Operational Flow — Why and How

**Lossless Compression Pipeline:**
- **Why:** Preserve all diagnostic information.
- **How:** Convert signal → remove statistical redundancy (predictive coding) → entropy-encode symbols (Huffman/Arithmetic) → bitstream.

**Lossy Compression Pipeline:**
- **Why:** Achieve high CR (10:1 to 50:1) where minor distortion is clinically acceptable.
- **How:** Transform signal (DCT/DWT) → quantize coefficients (information loss happens here) → entropy-encode → bitstream.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\displaystyle CR = \frac{N_{orig}}{N_{comp}}$ | $N_{orig}$ = original samples/bits, $N_{comp}$ = compressed samples/bits | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\displaystyle CR_{pct} = \left(1 - \frac{N_{comp}}{N_{orig}}\right) \times 100\%$ | Percentage space saved | % |
| 3 | $\displaystyle PRD = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{n=1}^{N}(x[n] - \hat{x}[n])^2}{\sum_{n=1}^{N} x[n]^2}} \times 100$ | $x[n]$ original, $\hat{x}[n]$ reconstructed | % |
| 4 | $\displaystyle SNR = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\sum x^2[n]}{\sum (x[n] - \hat{x}[n])^2}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dB |
| 5 | $\displaystyle bps = \frac{B_{total}}{N_{samples}}$ | Average bits per sample | bits/sample |
| 6 | $\displaystyle H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{M} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Shannon Entropy (theoretical lower bound) | bits/symbol |
| 7 | $\displaystyle \eta = \frac{H(X)}{\bar{L}}$ | Coding Efficiency ($\bar{L}$ = avg. code length) | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\displaystyle \sigma^2_{quant} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$ | Quantization noise variance (uniform quantizer, step $\Delta$) | V² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions / Quality Benchmarks (For KTU Answers):**
> - **PRD < 2%** → Excellent quality (clinically lossless).
> - **2% ≤ PRD < 5%** → Good quality, minor distortion.
> - **PRD ≥ 10%** → Noticeable distortion, may affect diagnosis.
> - **SNR > 30 dB** → Acceptable for diagnostic ECG.
> - **CR > 8** with **PRD < 5%** is considered a good lossy result.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication System):** Hospitals use **DICOM + JPEG 2000 (lossy/lossless modes)** to compress radiology images.
- **Holter Monitors & Implantable Devices:** Use **DWT-based lossy compression** to fit 24–48 hours of ECG in limited memory.
- **Telemedicine (KSWAN – Kerala State Wide Area Network):** Bandwidth constraints make lossy compression vital for rural ECG transmission.
- **AI/Deep Learning Pre-processing:** Compressed-domain features are increasingly used for direct CNN inference on medical data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Compression Ratio (CR)

**Statement:** Compression Ratio is the ratio of the original data size to the compressed data size.

**Step 1 — Define original data size:**
Let the original signal $x[n]$ have $N$ samples, each represented by $B$ bits per sample.

$$
N_{orig} = N \times B \quad \text{(bits)}
$$

**Step 2 — Define compressed data size:**
After compression, the bitstream contains $N_{comp}$ bits (which may include overhead for dictionaries/headers).

**Step 3 — Form the ratio:**

$$
CR = \frac{N_{orig}}{N_{comp}} = \frac{N \times B}{N_{comp}}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage Compression:**

$$
CR_{pct} = \left(1 - \frac{N_{comp}}{N_{orig}}\right) \times 100\%
$$

**Conversion logic:** A CR of 8 means the compressed file is 1/8th the size of the original, i.e., $CR_{pct} = 87.5\%$ space saved.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Percentage Root-mean-square Difference (PRD)

**Statement:** PRD quantifies the percentage error between the original and reconstructed signals.

**Step 1 — Define the per-sample error:**

$$
e[n] = x[n] - \hat{x}[n]
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mean squared error (numerator):**

$$
\text{Numerator} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} (x[n] - \hat{x}[n])^2 = \sum_{n=1}^{N} e^2[n]
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the signal energy (denominator):**

$$
\text{Denominator} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} x^2[n]
$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root and normalise to percentage:**

$$
PRD = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{n=1}^{N} e^2[n]}{\sum_{n=1}^{N} x^2[n]}} \times 100\%
$$

**Conversion logic:** A PRD of 0% means perfect reconstruction (lossless). Larger PRD = more distortion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modified PRD (PRD1)** is used in some texts to subtract the mean of $x[n]$ first, making it more robust to DC offset. The KTU 2024 scheme typically uses the standard PRD.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)

**Step 1 — Define signal power $P_s$ and noise power $P_n$:**

$$
P_s = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N} x^2[n], \qquad P_n = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N} (x[n] - \hat{x}[n])^2
$$

**Step 2 — Form the linear ratio and convert to dB:**

$$
SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_s}{P_n}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\sum x^2[n]}{\sum (x[n] - \hat{x}[n])^2}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Relate SNR to PRD:**
Substituting the PRD expression into the SNR formula yields:

$$
SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{(PRD/100)^2}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{100}{PRD}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;SNR_{dB} = 40 - 20\log_{10}(PRD)\;}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

Suppose an ECG signal of length $N = 1000$ samples is compressed using a DWT-threshold scheme.
- $\sum x^2[n] = 5.0 \times 10^{4}$
- $\sum (x[n] - \hat{x}[n])^2 = 1.25 \times 10^{2}$
- Compressed size = 25,000 bits; Original = 200,000 bits (8-bit, 1000 samples × extra header ignored)

**Compute CR:**

$$
CR = \frac{200{,}000}{25{,}000} = 8
$$

**Compute PRD:**

$$
PRD = \sqrt{\frac{1.25 \times 10^{2}}{5.0 \times 10^{4}}} \times 100 = \sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} \times 100 = 0.05 \times 100 = 5\%
$$

**Compute SNR:**

$$
SNR_{dB} = 40 - 20\log_{10}(5) = 40 - 20 \times 0.6990 = 40 - 13.98 = 26.02\;\text{dB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** CR = 8 with PRD = 5% and SNR ≈ 26 dB is a **clinically acceptable** lossy compression result for ECG signals.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Lossless vs Lossy

| Parameter | Lossless Compression | Lossy Compression |
|---|---|---|
| Reconstruction | **Exact** (bit-perfect) | **Approximate** (error introduced) |
| Typical CR | 2:1 to 4:1 | 8:1 to 50:1 |
| Distortion (PRD) | 0% | 1% – 10% |
| Algorithms | Huffman, Arithmetic, LZW, RLE | DCT, DWT + Quantization, VQ, CS |
| Computational cost | Lower | Higher (transforms + quantizer) |
| Clinical use | Diagnostic ECG, legal records | Telemedicine, archival, previews |
| Medical Standards | DICOM-lossless, PNG | JPEG, JPEG 2000, MPEG-medical |
| KTU Exam Weightage | High (definitions) | High (formula derivation) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic Block Diagram — Lossy vs Lossless Compression-Transmission-Reconstruction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["BIOMEDICAL SOURCE (ECG / EEG / MRI)"] --> acq["Analog / Digital Acquisition"]
    acq --> buf["Buffering and Preprocessing (Filtering, Normalisation)"]

    buf --> branch{Compression Type}
    branch -- "LOSSLESS PATH" --> ldec["Predictor / Decorrelator (e.g., DPCM)"]
    ldec --> lent["Entropy Encoder (Huffman / Arithmetic / LZW)"]
    lent --> lbit["Compressed Bitstream (Lossless)"]
    lbit --> lchan["Channel (Storage or Network)"]
    lchan --> ldec2["Entropy Decoder"]
    ldec2 --> lrec["Inverse Predictor"]
    lrec --> lout["Reconstructed Signal (EXACT MATCH)"]

    branch -- "LOSSY PATH" --> txfm["Transform (DCT / DWT / KLT)"]
    txfm --> qtz["Quantizer (Thresholding / Scalar / Vector)"]
    qtz --> ent["Entropy Encoder"]
    ent --> lbit
    lbit --> chan["Channel"]
    chan --> dec["Entropy Decoder"]
    dec --> deq["Inverse Quantizer (Approximate)"]
    deq --> itxfm["Inverse Transform"]
    itxfm --> out["Reconstructed Signal (PRD > 0)"]
```

### 4.2 Decision Flowchart — When to Use Lossless vs Lossy in Medical Context

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["New Medical Data to Compress"] --> q1{"Is data legally archived or diagnostic-critical?"}
    q1 -- "YES (ECG, biopsy, legal)" --> L1["Use LOSSLESS compression"]
    L1 --> L1a["Choose: Huffman / Arithmetic / DPCM / LZW"]
    L1a --> L1b["Acceptable CR 2:1 to 4:1, PRD = 0%"]
    L1b --> L1out["Store in PACS / DICOM-lossless"]

    q1 -- "NO" --> q2{"Is transmission over low-bandwidth link required?"}
    q2 -- "YES (Telemedicine)" --> L2["Use LOSSY compression"]
    L2 --> L2a["Choose: DWT-threshold / JPEG2000 / CS"]
    L2a --> L2b{"Does PRD remain < 5%?"}
    L2b -- "YES" --> L2c["Accept CR up to 20:1, transmit"]
    L2b -- "NO" --> L2d["Reduce CR or switch to lossless"]

    q2 -- "NO" --> L3["Use Archival Lossy (DWT + SPIHT)"]
    L3 --> L3out["CR 10:1 to 30:1, PRD 1-3%"]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — DWT-based Lossy ECG Compression (KTU Favourite)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ecg["ECG Signal x n"] --> dwt["DWT Decomposition (db4 / bior wavelet, 5 levels)"]
    dwt --> cA5["Approximation Coeff cA5"]
    dwt --> cD["Detail Coefficients cD1 to cD5"]
    cA5 --> thA["Threshold cA5 (low threshold)"]
    cD --> thD["Threshold cD1-cD5 (high threshold, hard thresholding)"]
    thA --> quant["Quantisation (Uniform Scalar)"]
    thD --> quant
    quant --> huff["Huffman / Run-length Encoding"]
    huff --> bit["Compressed Bitstream"]
    bit --> invHuff["Inverse Huffman"]
    invHuff --> dequant["Inverse Quantisation"]
    dequant --> invDWT["Inverse DWT (IDWT)"]
    invDWT --> rec["Reconstructed ECG x hat n"]
    rec --> prd["Compute PRD and CR"]
    ecg --> prd
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why DWT for ECG?**
> DWT captures both **time and frequency** information, making it ideal for non-stationary signals like ECG. Thresholding the **detail coefficients** (which mostly contain noise) yields high CR with minimal diagnostic loss — a key concept in KTU lab viva questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
*Define lossy and lossless compression. State two examples of each technique used in medical data compression.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Lossless compression** allows the original data to be reconstructed exactly without any loss. Examples: **Huffman coding**, **Arithmetic coding**, **LZW** (1.5 marks).
- **Lossy compression** allows controlled loss of information in exchange for higher compression ratios. Examples: **DCT-based JPEG**, **DWT + thresholding** (1.5 marks).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Style]**
*What is Compression Ratio (CR)? A 12-bit ECG signal of 5000 samples is compressed to 15,000 bits. Calculate the CR.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition:** CR is the ratio of the original data size to the compressed data size (1 mark).
- **Original size:** $N_{orig} = 5000 \times 12 = 60{,}000$ bits (1 mark).
- **Calculation:** $CR = \dfrac{60{,}000}{15{,}000} = 4$ (1 mark).

---

### PART B — Full 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following two questions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the block diagram of a DWT-based lossy ECG compression system. Discuss the role of thresholding and quantisation in achieving high compression ratio with acceptable PRD.*

**(b) [7 Marks)** *For an EEG signal of length N = 2000 samples sampled at 12 bits/sample, the compressed bitstream is 12,000 bits. If the mean squared error between original and reconstructed EEG is 8.0 and the original signal energy is 8000, compute (i) Compression Ratio, (ii) PRD, and (iii) SNR in dB. Comment on the clinical acceptability of the result.*

**Model Solution (a):**
- Draw/describe the **DWT block diagram** (Section 4.3) (2 marks).
- Explain **multi-resolution decomposition**, choice of mother wavelet (db4 / biorthogonal), and level selection (3 to 5) (2 marks).
- Explain **thresholding strategy**: hard vs soft thresholding; preserve approximation coefficients (low-frequency diagnostic content), aggressively threshold detail coefficients (1.5 marks).
- Explain **quantisation** as the information-loss step; mention $\sigma^2_{quant} = \Delta^2/12$ (1.5 marks).

**Model Solution (b):**
- Original size: $N_{orig} = 2000 \times 12 = 24{,}000$ bits.
- **(i) CR** $= \dfrac{24{,}000}{12{,}000} = 2$ (2 marks).
- **(ii) PRD** $= \sqrt{\dfrac{8.0}{8000}} \times 100 = \sqrt{0.001} \times 100 = 1\% \times 100 = 3.16\%$ (3 marks).
  - Correction: $PRD = \sqrt{0.001} \times 100 = 0.0316 \times 100 = 3.16\%$ (full marks).
- **(iii) SNR** $= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{8000}{8}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(1000) = 30$ dB (2 marks).
- **Clinical comment:** CR = 2 (low, near-lossless territory), PRD = 3.16% (good), SNR = 30 dB (acceptable). Result is **clinically acceptable for diagnostic EEG** (1 mark).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Compare and contrast lossless and lossy compression techniques for medical data. Tabulate the differences in terms of CR, PRD, computational complexity, and clinical application with at least 6 parameters.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A 10-second ECG sampled at 360 Hz with 11-bit resolution is compressed using Huffman coding. The original file size is 39,600 bits. The Huffman table produces an average code length of 4.2 bits/sample. Calculate (i) the compressed file size, (ii) Compression Ratio, (iii) Percentage space saved, (iv) Coding Efficiency if the entropy of the source is 3.8 bits/symbol.*

**Model Solution (a):**
- Tabular comparison with **at least 6 parameters** (CR, PRD, complexity, use case, algorithms, standard) — 4 marks.
- Brief narrative explanation of **why lossless is mandatory for diagnostic data** and **why lossy is preferred for telemedicine** — 2 marks.
- Mention of **DICOM** standard supporting both modes — 1 mark.

**Model Solution (b):**
- Number of samples: $N = 360 \times 10 = 3600$ samples.
- Original size: $N_{orig} = 3600 \times 11 = 39{,}600$ bits (confirms).
- **(i) Compressed size:** $N_{comp} = 3600 \times 4.2 = 15{,}120$ bits (2 marks).
- **(ii) CR:** $\dfrac{39{,}600}{15{,}120} = 2.62$ (2 marks).
- **(iii) Space saved:** $\left(1 - \dfrac{15{,}120}{39{,}600}\right)\times 100\% = 61.82\%$ (1.5 marks).
- **(iv) Coding Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{H(X)}{\bar{L}} = \dfrac{3.8}{4.2} = 0.905$ or **90.5%** (1.5 marks).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by N in PRD formula:** Many students write $PRD = \sqrt{\frac{x^2 - \hat{x}^2}{x^2}}$ — **WRONG.** Always use the sum $\sum (x[n] - \hat{x}[n])^2$ over the signal.
> 2. **Confusing CR and Compression Percentage:** CR = 8 means 87.5% saved, not 8% saved. **Read the question carefully.**
> 3. **Mixing up bps (bits per sample) and CR:** $bps = B_{total}/N$ is the *bit rate*, not the ratio.
> 4. **Forgetting the ×100%** in PRD — dimensionless form is *not* a percentage; examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 5. **In lossy compression, do not claim PRD = 0.** It is always > 0 except in the trivial case of zero signal.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Lossless** = **EXACT** reconstruction; **Lossy** = **APPROXIMATE** reconstruction.
- ✅ **CR** = $N_{orig}/N_{comp}$; **Always > 1** if compression is achieved.
- ✅ **PRD** = 0% for lossless; **PRD < 5%** is the clinical benchmark for "good" lossy ECG.
- ✅ **SNR (dB)** = $10\log_{10}(\text{Signal Power} / \text{Noise Power})$; **> 30 dB** is diagnostic-grade.
- ✅ **Key Relationship:** $SNR_{dB} = 40 - 20\log_{10}(PRD)$ — *memorise this*.
- ✅ **DWT** is preferred over DCT for ECG/EEG because of time-frequency localisation.
- ✅ **Thresholding + Quantisation** is the **only lossy step** in a transform coder; entropy coding itself is lossless.
- ✅ **DICOM** is the universal medical imaging standard supporting both lossless (JPEG-LS, RLE) and lossy (JPEG, JPEG 2000) modes.
- ✅ **DPCM** = simple, low-complexity lossless ECG coder; foundation for ADPCM.
- ✅ **Compressed Sensing (CS)** is a modern lossy technique exploiting sparsity — emerging research area.
- ✅ **Quality vs. Compression trade-off** is the *central theme* of any medical data compression answer.
- ✅ **Viva question:** "Why not always use lossless?" → Answer: Storage, bandwidth, and energy constraints in implantable/remote devices force us to accept controlled loss.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
